meta.title: APM Foundations: 21-Day PM Competency Roadmap
meta.framework: Reforge 12 PM Competency Model by Ravi Mehta

preReadingBeforeDay1.title: Behind Every Great Product — Marty Cagan, SVPG
preReadingBeforeDay1.why: 10-minute read that answers 'why does the PM role exist at all?' — having this context makes Day 1 framework reading land much harder.

days.theme: What the PM role actually is
days.objective: Map all 12 Reforge PM competencies to real PM responsibilities and explain — in writing — what an APM at Swiggy does differently from the engineering lead, the designer, and the data analyst on the same squad.

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy operates with dedicated product squads — one each for the consumer app, restaurant partner tools, logistics, and growth — where APMs are embedded in a single squad and own specific feature areas end-to-end alongside engineers, designers, and analysts.

days.teamContext: Today's topic touches all four cross-functional partners — Engineering (who builds), Design (who shapes UX), Data/Analytics (who measures), and Marketing/Growth (who acquires users) — because understanding the PM role requires seeing clearly how it differs from each of these functions.

tasks.title: Product Manager Skills by Seniority Level — Ravi Mehta, Reforge
tasks.summary: Ravi Mehta, former VP of Product at Tinder and author of the Reforge PM competency model, maps the 12 core PM competencies across three seniority levels — APM, PM, and Senior PM. This is the foundational framework for the entire 21-day roadmap; reading it on Day 1 gives Pranav a mental map of every skill he will build over the next three weeks.
keyTakeaways: The 12 competencies are organised into four pillars — Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People — and each pillar matters at the APM level, though execution and customer insight are weighted more heavily than strategy and influence early in a PM career
APMs who outperform in Product Execution and Customer Insight get promoted; those who lead with strategy before building execution credibility often lose team trust first
Being 'product-minded' means every feature decision is connected to a user outcome AND a business metric — this is what separates a PM from a project manager or a business analyst

tasks.title: The Product Manager — Marty Cagan, SVPG
tasks.summary: Marty Cagan, founder of SVPG and author of Inspired, defines what a product manager is responsible for — and critically, what they are NOT responsible for — in a product-led company. This gives Pranav a second lens alongside Reforge: where Reforge is a skills and seniority map, SVPG is a responsibilities and accountability map.
keyTakeaways: The PM's core job is to discover a product that is simultaneously valuable (users want it), usable (users can use it), feasible (engineering can build it), and viable (the business should build it) — all four tests apply to every feature, not just new products

A PM does not own design, engineering, or data — but they are fully accountable for the outcome those teams produce together, which is a fundamentally different relationship than a manager or a consultant
The biggest failure mode for APMs is becoming a feature factory — executing a list of requests from stakeholders rather than discovering the right problem to solve first

tasks.keyword: Swiggy APM associate product manager day in life squad structure India 2024
tasks.whyItMatters: Reading frameworks gives you vocabulary; seeing how a real Indian APM spends their actual day gives you context for applying that vocabulary in practice. Look for blog posts, LinkedIn articles, or YouTube videos by Swiggy, Zomato, or Zepto PMs describing their daily workflow — focus on squad structure, weekly rituals (standups, design reviews, data reviews, stakeholder syncs), and how they handle competing priorities.
whatToLearn: What percentage of a PM's week is in meetings versus solo work — and what kinds of meetings they run (sprint planning, design review, data review, escalation calls, roadmap presentations)
How an Indian product team at a consumer internet company divides ownership between APM and PM — what the APM owns independently and what requires PM or leadership sign-off

tasks.promptTitle: Map 15 Swiggy PM tasks to the 4 Reforge pillars
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM coach helping me build a foundational understanding of the PM role using the Reforge 12-competency model.

Today I studied:
- Ravi Mehta's 12 PM competencies across 4 pillars: Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, Influencing People
- Marty Cagan's definition of the PM's core job: discover a product that is valuable, usable, feasible, and viable
- How Swiggy organises its product team into squads with APMs embedded in specific feature areas

Real world context: Imagine I am an APM at Swiggy's consumer app squad. My squad owns the homepage feed, cart, and checkout experience.

Your role today: Test whether I understand what a PM actually does by having me categorise real PM tasks. Push back if I put something in the wrong pillar. Ask me WHY for every answer. Do not move on until I have justified my choice.

Activity — 15 PM tasks to categorise:
Below are 15 tasks from a typical Swiggy APM's week. I will tell you which of the 4 Reforge pillars each task belongs to. For each one, ask me to justify my answer before confirming. After all 15, give me a score and tell me where I was confused.

1. Write acceptance criteria for a new restaurant sorting algorithm
2. Review a Mixpanel funnel showing drop-off at the payment step
3. Interview 5 Swiggy users about why they abandon their cart
4. Write a product requirements document for a 'reorder last meal' button
5. Align with the engineering lead on sprint scope for next week
6. Present the Q3 product roadmap to the VP of Product
7. Define the North Star metric for the checkout squad
8. Review a designer's wireframe for the new address confirmation screen
9. Identify that app ratings dropped 0.3 stars after the last release
10. Make the case to leadership for prioritising checkout speed over new features
11. Run a team retrospective with engineering and design
12. Analyse a competitor app (Zomato) for differences in the checkout flow
13. Write a 1-pager on why Swiggy should build offline ordering for low-connectivity areas
14. Tell the data analyst exactly what query to run and why
15. Negotiate scope trade-offs with the engineering lead when the sprint is overloaded

After I respond to all 15, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about how Indian PM roles at consumer internet companies differ from US/global norms — especially the cross-functional dynamics at Swiggy, Zepto, or Razorpay]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Let's go task by task. Start with Task 1: Write acceptance criteria for a new restaurant sorting algorithm. Which of the 4 Reforge pillars does this belong to — and why?'

bonusTasks.title: Good Product Team / Failed Product Team — Marty Cagan, SVPG
bonusTasks.summary: Cagan's most-shared article draws a sharp contrast between empowered product teams (who own outcomes) and feature factories (who execute requests). Reading this after Day 1's core tasks reframes the PM role as a cultural and organisational choice — critical context for APMs joining large companies like Swiggy or Razorpay where both team types can exist in the same building.

days.theme: Structured thinking — MECE and first principles
days.objective: Build a correctly structured MECE tree for a real product problem, place 6 provided causes into the correct branches without overlap, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and challenge one provided assumption using first-principles reasoning.

realWorldAnchor.example: When Zepto evaluated expanding from metro dark stores into Tier 2 cities, the decision required first-principles reasoning — not 'Blinkit is doing it so we should too' but a ground-up decomposition of whether unit economics, demand density, and supply chain assumptions that held in Mumbai would hold in Nagpur.

days.teamContext: Structured thinking is most visible in the PM's interactions with Engineering leads during problem scoping — when a PM can decompose a problem into mutually exclusive branches, engineering scope becomes cleaner, trade-offs become explicit, and design priorities become obvious to the whole team.

tasks.title: MECE: The Framework That Makes You Think Like a Consultant — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the MECE principle (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) as a thinking tool specifically for product managers — how to decompose problems into branches that have no overlap and no gaps. For Pranav, who has consulting exposure, MECE will feel structurally familiar, but this article adds the product-specific application: breaking down a metric drop, a user complaint spike, or a prioritisation trade-off.
keyTakeaways: MECE means every branch of your breakdown is non-overlapping (mutually exclusive) AND together the branches cover every possibility without exception (collectively exhaustive) — violating either rule produces a flawed analysis that will mislead the team

In PM work, MECE trees are most useful in three situations: diagnosing a metric drop (what could have caused this?), segmenting a user base for feature prioritisation, and structuring a product strategy recommendation to leadership

The hardest discipline in MECE is avoiding the intuitive jump to solutions — your tree branches must describe the problem space completely before you touch solutions, otherwise you will solve the wrong branch

tasks.title: First Principles Thinking — Farnam Street
tasks.summary: Farnam Street's definitive explainer on first principles reasoning — the method of breaking a problem down to its most fundamental truths and building back up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy from what others have done. This is the thinking method behind Jio's free data launch, Zepto's 10-minute delivery model, and CRED's credit-score-only user filter — each was a first-principles bet made by questioning an assumption everyone else accepted.
keyTakeaways: Reasoning by analogy ('Swiggy did X so we should do X') is fast but caps innovation — first principles forces the question: is this assumption actually true, or have we inherited it from someone who inherited it from someone else?

A practical first-principles test for PMs has three questions: (1) Is this actually true? (2) Is it always true regardless of context? (3) Does it have to be true, or is it only true given current constraints that might change?

Jio's 2016 decision to give away free data at scale is the most cited Indian business example of first principles — Mukesh Ambani questioned the assumption that data must be priced as a premium good, concluded it was false given Jio's cost structure, and restructured Indian telecom permanently

tasks.keyword: MECE tree metric drop product manager worked example diagnosis walkthrough PM interview
tasks.whyItMatters: Reading about MECE is the first step; seeing a PM use MECE to walk through a specific metric drop — with the full tree drawn out, branches named, and a hypothesis selected — is where the concept becomes usable. Look for blog walkthroughs or YouTube videos that show the tree visually, not just explain it abstractly.
whatToLearn: What the top-level branches of a metric drop MECE tree typically look like — the most common structures are: internal vs external causes; supply-side vs demand-side; technical vs behavioural; or a funnel-stage breakdown (acquisition / activation / retention / revenue)

How a PM selects which branch to investigate first — the answer combines three factors: which is most likely given available context, which is fastest to verify with existing data, and which has the biggest business impact if the hypothesis is confirmed

tasks.promptTitle: Build a MECE tree for Zomato's Monday order drop
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM coach helping me practice MECE structured thinking for product problem diagnosis.

Today I studied:
- The MECE principle: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive — used to decompose problems without gaps or overlaps
- First principles reasoning: breaking assumptions down to fundamental truths rather than reasoning by analogy
- How Zepto used first principles to evaluate Tier 2 expansion, and how Jio used it to restructure Indian telecom pricing assumptions

Real world context: Zomato's Monday morning order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. The CEO wants an explanation by end of day. No additional data has been provided yet.

Your role today: Act as my skeptical PM mentor. When I build my MECE tree, challenge me on every branch — is this truly mutually exclusive from the other branches? Does the tree cover everything plausible? Have I missed an entire category? Push me to think one level deeper, not just validate my first answer.

Activity — 3 rounds:
Round 1: I state my top-level branches (2-4 categories) and explain why they are MECE.
Round 2: For the branch I identify as most likely, I expand it into sub-branches.
Round 3: I pick the most investigable sub-branch and name 3 specific data checks I would run.

After all 3 rounds, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Monday order patterns in India are shaped by specific local factors: weekly prepaid recharge cycles, salary credit dates, weather patterns affecting delivery areas, and Monday-after-a-holiday effects — a non-India PM might not know to ask about these]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Before you build the tree — tell me in one sentence: what question is your MECE tree trying to answer? Then give me your top-level branches.'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Build a complete 2-level MECE tree for Zomato's 22% Monday order drop, place all 6 provided causes into the correct branches, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and use first-principles reasoning to challenge one of the 6 provided assumptions.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Zomato's Monday order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. Here are 6 possible causes flagged by different team members:

1. The payment gateway had 400ms higher latency on Monday morning (flagged by engineering)
2. It rained heavily in Delhi and Mumbai on Sunday night (flagged by operations)
3. Zomato ran a 40% discount campaign last Monday but not this Monday (flagged by marketing)
4. Three popular restaurant chains in Mumbai went offline simultaneously (flagged by restaurant partnerships)
5. A competitor (Swiggy) launched a 'Free Delivery Monday' promo this week (flagged by growth team)
6. The iOS app had a crash on the restaurant search screen affecting users on app version 14.2 (flagged by engineering)

Your deliverables:
A. Build a MECE tree with at least 2 levels. Top-level branches must cover all 6 causes above without any overlap. Place each of the 6 causes under the correct branch — no cause should appear in two branches.
B. Identify which top-level branch you would investigate first. Justify in 3 sentences using: likelihood given available context, speed of verification, and business impact if true.
C. Using first-principles reasoning, identify ONE assumption in the list above that you would question before accepting as fact. State what you would verify and why the assumption might be wrong.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part A: A visual tree using indentation or dashes — top-level branches clearly labelled, sub-branches indented, each of the 6 causes placed at the leaf level with its number. Part B: Three sentences of justification (one each for: likelihood, verification speed, business impact). Part C: One assumption named + a 2-sentence explanation of why you would verify it before accepting it.

days.theme: User thinking — Personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done
days.objective: Write a specific product persona from a real app store complaint, identify the underlying Job To Be Done using the correct JTBD format, and propose one feature with a measurable success metric.

realWorldAnchor.example: CRED's decision to restrict membership exclusively to users with credit scores above 750 was not a technical constraint — it was a deliberate user segmentation choice based on a specific JTBD: 'help me feel recognised and rewarded for the financial discipline I already practise,' which Kunal Shah believed only high-credit-score users would authentically experience.

days.teamContext: Personas and JTBD are most directly used in the PM's collaboration with the Design team — PMs use these frameworks to align designers on exactly who they are building for, preventing the most common design failure: building for an abstract average user rather than a specific person in a specific situation.

tasks.title: Jobs-to-be-Done: A Framework for Customer Needs — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains Clayton Christensen's Jobs-To-Be-Done framework for product managers — the idea that users do not buy products or use features; they hire them to accomplish a specific job in their life. For Pranav, JTBD reframes the question from 'what features should we build?' to 'what job is the user trying to get done, and are we the best tool for that job?' — a shift that changes every prioritisation and design decision downstream.
keyTakeaways: The classic JTBD example: people do not buy a drill — they hire it to make a hole, because they need a shelf mounted before guests arrive. The real job is the shelf, not the hole. A PM who understands the real job might build a modular shelf system instead of a better drill

JTBD replaces demographic personas with situational ones: not 'a 28-year-old urban professional' but 'someone who just moved into a new flat and needs it to feel like home before the weekend' — the situation, not the age bracket, drives the job

For Indian fintech (CRED, PhonePe, Razorpay), JTBD forces the question: are users hiring the payment app to transact efficiently, to feel in control of their money, or to be rewarded for behaviour they already practise? Each is a different job and leads to a completely different product

tasks.title: How to Create User Personas That Drive Real Product Decisions — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how to build a useful product persona — not a marketing persona full of demographics, but a product persona focused on the user's goals, pain points, mental models, and behaviours relevant to the product. The article distinguishes between proto-personas (hypothesis-based) and research-backed personas, and explains when each is appropriate for different stages of product development.
keyTakeaways: A useful product persona has four non-negotiable components: who they are in context (not demographics), what they want to accomplish (goal), what blocks them (pain), and how they currently cope (existing workaround) — missing any one makes the persona useless for design decisions

Demographic personas ('Priya, 27, Mumbai, IT sector') are nearly useless for product decisions; situational personas ('Priya is trying to split a dinner bill quickly without awkwardness while standing at the restaurant counter') drive real design choices because they specify the exact moment of friction

At companies like Zepto or CRED, a PM typically maintains 2-3 primary personas — more than three and the team loses focus; fewer than two and you miss critical use case differences that will cause one segment to churn

tasks.keyword: CRED Kunal Shah user segmentation 750 credit score product strategy reasoning interview 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: CRED's decision to restrict membership to users with 750+ credit scores is a real, publicly debated product and strategy call that Kunal Shah has explained in interviews and talks — searching for his reasoning gives Pranav a master class in deliberate user segmentation, the antithesis of 'build for everyone.' Look for podcast interviews, LinkedIn posts, or long-form articles where Kunal Shah explains the CRED user thesis in his own words.
whatToLearn: Why restricting your user base can be a strategic product advantage rather than a growth ceiling — and the specific conditions under which this logic holds versus when it becomes a self-imposed constraint that limits scale

How Kunal Shah used JTBD-style thinking ('what job does a financially disciplined user want done that no product is currently serving well?') to define CRED's initial product direction and resist pressure to expand to lower credit score users

tasks.promptTitle: Build persona, JTBD, and feature proposal from a Zepto complaint
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice translating real user complaints into actionable product personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done statements.

Today I studied:
- JTBD framework: users hire products to accomplish a situational job — the job is defined by the situation, not the demographic
- Product persona structure: context (who they are in situation), goal (what they want), pain (what blocks them), existing workaround (how they cope today)
- CRED's deliberate user segmentation: only users with 750+ credit scores, because the JTBD was 'feel recognised for financial discipline already practised'

Real world context: I am a PM on Zepto's consumer app squad. I have just received a batch of App Store reviews. My job is to extract a persona, a JTBD, and a feature proposal from a specific review.

Your role today: Act as a PM mentor who pushes me to go deeper. When I write a persona, ask: 'What would you actually build for this person — and what would you NOT build?' When I write a JTBD, ask: 'Is that the real job, or is it a symptom of a deeper job?' Do not accept surface-level answers.

Activity:

Here are 5 Zepto App Store reviews:

Review 1 (1 star): 'Charged me twice for one order. Customer care took 3 days to refund. Never using again.'
Review 2 (2 stars): 'Good app but delivery is often late. 10 minutes means 25 minutes in my area.'
Review 3 (3 stars): 'I use Zepto every day for my morning groceries but I have to retype the same items every single time. There is no saved list or easy reorder. So annoying for someone who buys the same things.'
Review 4 (4 stars): 'Fast delivery, great selection. Wish there were more organic options.'
Review 5 (2 stars): 'The app keeps crashing on my phone (Redmi Note 11). Very frustrating.'

We will work on Review 3 only.

Step 1: I write a persona for the user behind Review 3. You push back if I use demographics without situational context, or if any of the four components is missing or vague.
Step 2: I write a JTBD statement in this exact format: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation/goal], so I can [expected outcome].' You push back if the job is surface-level.
Step 3: I propose one specific Zepto feature that directly addresses the JTBD. I include a success metric in the format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].'

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — daily grocery buying in Indian households follows a very different pattern than Western weekly grocery shopping: the ration list, the kirana store mental model, the household structure where the same 15-20 staples are bought on a near-daily basis — Zepto's repeat-purchase problem has specific Indian household context]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Good. Start with Step 1. Write me a persona for the user behind Review 3. Remember: context in their situation, goal, pain, existing workaround. No demographics unless they are directly relevant to the product decision. Go.'

bonusTasks.title: Why Dunzo failed — JTBD analysis
bonusTasks.keyword: Dunzo failure reasons product strategy user value proposition India 2023 2024 post-mortem on-demand delivery
bonusTasks.summary: Dunzo was once considered a pioneer of on-demand delivery in India but failed to achieve the scale of Swiggy, Zepto, or Blinkit despite an early head start. Analysing its failure through the JTBD lens — did users have a clearly defined job they were hiring Dunzo for? Was that job valuable enough to pay for without subsidies? — gives Pranav a real counter-example to CRED's sharp user segmentation. Look for post-mortems on inc42.com, Mint, The Ken, or Finshots.

assessmentTask.rawMaterial:

Review 1 (1 star): 'Charged me twice for one order. Customer care took 3 days to refund. Never using again.'
Review 2 (2 stars): 'Good app but delivery is often late. 10 minutes means 25 minutes in my area.'
Review 3 (3 stars): 'I use Zepto every day for my morning groceries but I have to retype the same items every single time. There is no saved list or easy reorder. So annoying for someone who buys the same things.'

days.theme: Problem framing and user research
days.objective: Reframe three vague problem statements into properly scoped statements using all four required components, and write a 5-question user interview script for one of them using only open-ended, behaviour-based questions.

realWorldAnchor.example: Razorpay's product team reportedly reframed payment failures from 'a technical reliability problem' to 'a trust breakdown at the exact moment a user most needs the product to work' — this reframe shifted the solution space from purely infrastructure fixes to UX interventions: real-time failure explanations, instant retry flows, and visible settlement timelines that rebuilt user confidence.

days.teamContext: Problem framing is where the PM's relationship with the Data team becomes most critical — a well-scoped problem statement tells the analyst exactly what data to pull and what anomaly to look for, while a vague problem statement results in weeks of back-and-forth pulling the wrong metrics.

tasks.title: How to Write a Product Problem Statement — Product School
tasks.summary: This article walks through how to write a product problem statement that is specific enough to be actionable — covering the four required components (user in situation, goal, pain, business impact) and showing examples of vague versus well-scoped statements side by side. For Pranav, this directly bridges yesterday's JTBD work with today's skill: a JTBD describes the job the user wants done; a problem statement describes the gap between the current product and that job being done well.
keyTakeaways: A problem statement requires four components: who the user is in a specific situation (not a demographic), what they are trying to accomplish (specific goal), what is blocking them (specific friction or pain), and why it matters to the business (a metric or outcome that is affected) — missing any one makes it unactionable for engineering and design

Vague problem statements generate vague solutions: 'users are not engaging' has infinite possible solutions; 'first-time UPI users in Tier 2 cities abandon at the payment confirmation screen because they distrust whether the payment went through' generates a specific, testable intervention

The business impact component is the most commonly skipped — but without it, the PM cannot prioritise this problem against competing problems, and engineers and designers have no sense of urgency or strategic relevance

tasks.title: How to Run Effective User Interviews — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky covers how to design and run user interviews that produce genuine behavioural insight rather than confirmation of existing hypotheses. The article covers question design, interview structure, and how to synthesize findings into actionable product insights — a practitioner-level guide that APMs can apply to user calls starting this week.
keyTakeaways: The most common interview mistake is asking 'would you use this feature?' — users almost always say yes to hypotheticals because they want to be helpful. Instead ask: 'Tell me about the last time you tried to do X. What actually happened?' — behaviour-based questions reveal real patterns, not ideals
Five well-run user interviews produce more actionable insight than 50 survey responses — qualitative interviews reveal the 'why' behind the 'what' that surveys can only count

In Indian markets, user interview design requires two additional decisions: language choice (English vs vernacular) and setting (in-person vs phone), because self-reported behaviour in formal English interviews often differs substantially from actual usage behaviour in the product's natural context

days.theme: Prioritisation frameworks — RICE, ICE, and MoSCoW
days.objective: Score 8 PhonePe features using the RICE formula with full arithmetic working shown for each, rank all 8, select the top 3 for a pre-Diwali launch with justification, and give one-sentence business reasoning for dropping each of the remaining 5.

realWorldAnchor.example: Before a Diwali or IPL season launch, a Swiggy PM runs through the backlog using a prioritisation framework to decide which features get engineering time — festive seasons represent disproportionate revenue and user acquisition in India, meaning the Reach and Confidence inputs in RICE shift significantly for features that serve high-volume moments.

days.teamContext: Prioritisation decisions directly involve Engineering (who estimates Effort in person-months) and Marketing/Growth (who provides Reach estimates and impact context for campaign-linked features) — the PM's job is to synthesize inputs from both teams into a scored framework, not to decide unilaterally.

tasks.title: RICE: Simple Prioritization for Product Managers — Intercom
tasks.summary: This is the original blog post from Intercom that introduced and defined the RICE scoring model — Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort. Intercom's PM team created RICE to solve a specific internal problem: too many competing priorities and no shared scoring language across teams. The article explains the formula precisely, defines each variable with examples, and shows worked calculations. This is the definitive primary source for the framework.
keyTakeaways: RICE Score = (Reach × Impact × Confidence%) divided by Effort in person-months — the formula must be applied consistently across all features being compared, otherwise the resulting scores cannot be ranked against each other

Confidence is the most underused and most important variable: it forces honesty about how well the team actually knows the Reach and Impact estimates, preventing overconfident bets on features that are exciting but untested

RICE works best for comparing features within the same product area and timeframe — it breaks down when comparing a speculative 0-to-1 feature (low confidence, high potential) against an incremental improvement (high confidence, modest impact), because those are fundamentally different types of bets

tasks.title: How to Prioritize Product Features — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky surveys how top product teams at companies like Airbnb, Figma, and Duolingo actually prioritise features — not just what framework they use, but how they balance quantitative scoring with strategic judgment and when they override the numbers. The article is especially useful for understanding the limits of RICE and the most common prioritisation mistakes.
keyTakeaways: No framework replaces strategic judgment: a feature with a high RICE score that contradicts the company's current strategic focus should still be deprioritised — frameworks inform decisions, they do not make them

The three most common RICE mistakes: treating effort estimates from engineers as precise (they are wide estimates), forgetting that Reach estimates decay as the product matures, and conflating impact on users with impact on the specific business metric you are trying to move this quarter

MoSCoW (Must Have / Should Have / Could Have / Won't Have) is most useful for scoping a specific release, not for cross-feature portfolio prioritisation — use RICE to decide what to build across the quarter, use MoSCoW to decide what ships in the next sprint

tasks.keyword: RICE prioritization worked example product manager feature scoring table India startup walkthrough
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing a PM walk through RICE on a realistic feature list — with actual numbers in a table, arithmetic shown, and final rankings justified — is where the framework becomes something Pranav can use rather than just describe. Look for blog posts or PM prep resources that show a full scoring table with working, not just the formula definition.
whatToLearn: How experienced PMs estimate Reach when exact data is not available — the acceptable approximation methods (monthly active users × estimated feature touch rate), and how to be explicit about assumptions so they can be challenged

What a final RICE output looks like structurally: a table with all features ranked by score, followed by a short narrative justifying the top choices and explaining why high-scoring features were still dropped for strategic reasons

days.theme: Product design frameworks — CIRCLES and BUS
days.objective: Produce a complete written CIRCLES answer for a CRED product design case covering all 7 steps, with a specific user definition, India-specific reasoning at each stage, and a clear recommendation in the final summary step.

realWorldAnchor.example: CRED's product design philosophy prioritises aesthetics and reward-loop psychology over feature richness — any design answer for CRED must begin by defining who the user is (the financially responsible, aesthetically oriented, 750+ credit score user) because CRED's design choices are a direct expression of that specific user's mental model and aspirations.

days.teamContext: CIRCLES is most directly relevant to the PM's collaboration with the Design team — it provides a shared language for working through a design decision end-to-end, and prevents the most common failure mode in design reviews: jumping to features without first aligning on users, goals, and success criteria.

tasks.title: The CIRCLES Method: A Product Design Framework — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the CIRCLES method — Comprehend the situation, Identify the customer, Report the customer's needs, Cut through prioritisation, List solutions, Evaluate trade-offs, Summarise your recommendation — as the primary framework for answering product design questions in PM interviews and running internal design discussions. This is the core framework for Day 6 and will be used directly in the assessment.
keyTakeaways: CIRCLES is not a rigid checklist — it is a thinking sequence that prevents the two most common PM design failures: jumping to features without defining the user (skipping I and R), and proposing solutions without stating trade-offs (skipping E)

The most important and most skipped step is C — Comprehend the situation: always begin by asking clarifying questions about the goal, constraints, and context before touching users or features; PM interviewers consistently report this as the single biggest differentiator between strong and weak candidates

In Indian PM interviews, CIRCLES requires an additional dimension: at step I (Identify the customer), always check whether your user definition covers both urban English-first users and Tier 2 vernacular users — failure to segment often means your solution works for only the easier half of the Indian market

tasks.title: How to Answer Product Design Questions in PM Interviews — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky compiles advice from PM interviewers and hiring managers on what separates strong from weak product design answers — covering not just what to say but how to say it: thinking out loud, handling clarifying questions under pressure, demonstrating user empathy through specificity, and managing time across the 7 CIRCLES steps.
keyTakeaways: The biggest differentiator in design answers is demonstrating user empathy through specificity — 'a 34-year-old CRED user who wants to understand what is pulling her credit score down after adding a new credit card' is more impressive than 'urban working professionals,' because it shows real user thinking

Interviewers consistently score reasoning process higher than the final answer — a wrong recommendation with clear, stepwise logical reasoning scores higher than a correct answer with fuzzy or missing reasoning

Time management guidance for CIRCLES: spend roughly 25% of your time on C/I/R, 20% on Cut, 35% on List and Evaluate, and 20% on Summarise — most candidates rush the user definition and spend too long listing features

tasks.keyword: CIRCLES method CRED credit score feature product design answer PM interview walkthrough India fintech
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing the CIRCLES framework applied to a product with a niche, well-defined user segment — specifically an Indian fintech with premium positioning and a specific credit score user — prepares Pranav to apply it to the exact CRED case in his assessment. Look for PM interview prep videos or written walkthroughs where a candidate works through CIRCLES on a credit or financial product design question.
whatToLearn: How candidates handle the 'Identify the customer' step when the product has a deliberately narrow user segment — for CRED, the 750+ credit score user is not just a demographic filter but a proxy for specific values (financial discipline, status consciousness, aesthetic sensitivity) that should directly inform every feature suggestion

What a strong CIRCLES summary step sounds like — one paragraph that connects the recommended solution back to the user's primary need, the company's strategic positioning, and 2 key trade-offs that were evaluated and rejected

tasks.promptTitle: Live CIRCLES walkthrough — PM interview simulation for CRED credit score feature
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM interviewer running a live product design interview with me as a candidate for an APM role at CRED.

Case question:

bonusTasks.promptTitle: Apply BUS framework to the same CRED case — compare with CIRCLES
bonusTasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me compare two product design frameworks: CIRCLES and BUS (Business goal, User goal, Success metrics) using the same CRED case.

I have already completed a full CIRCLES answer for this case: 'Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score.'

Your role today: Guide me through the same case using BUS. BUS is simpler and faster than CIRCLES — it is the framework used in internal design reviews to align a team quickly on what is being built and why, before going into full feature design.

BUS structure:
B — Business goal: What does CRED want to achieve with this feature? Pick the most important one and justify it.
U — User goal: What specific job does the CRED user want done? Write it in JTBD format: 'When [situation], I want to [goal], so I can [outcome].'
S — Success metrics: Name 2-3 metrics that would tell you this feature is working, and 1 guardrail metric that would tell you it is not.

Activity: I answer B, then U, then S. After each answer, you push back or confirm as a senior PM would in a design review.

After both frameworks are complete, ask me: 'What did CIRCLES give you that BUS did not? What did BUS give you that CIRCLES did not? When would you use each in a real product team context?'

Begin with: 'Let's run BUS on the CRED credit score feature. Start with B — Business goal. What is CRED trying to achieve with a credit score education feature? Think about their retention challenges, their monetisation model, and their competitive positioning before you answer.'

tasks.title: The Four Big Risks — Marty Cagan, SVPG
tasks.summary: Marty Cagan's framework for product risk — value risk (will users want it?), usability risk (can users use it?), feasibility risk (can engineering build it?), and viability risk (should the business build it?) — provides the integration lens for Day 7. Every tool from Week 1 maps onto one or more of these risks: JTBD and personas address value risk, CIRCLES addresses usability risk, RICE addresses viability risk, and PM-engineering collaboration addresses feasibility. This reading shows how Week 1's toolkit is a coherent risk management system, not a collection of unrelated frameworks.
keyTakeaways: Most product failures happen because one of these four risks was discovered too late — after significant engineering investment, not before it. The PM's job is to reduce all four risks before committing resources, which is why discovery work matters more than execution speed

Value risk and usability risk are addressed through user research and design thinking (JTBD, personas, CIRCLES). Feasibility risk requires close PM-engineering collaboration. Viability risk requires the PM to understand the business model well enough to say no to things that don't move the right metrics

For APMs at Indian consumer internet companies, viability risk is the most commonly underweighted — APMs with strong user empathy sometimes over-index on value and usability while underestimating unit economics constraints specific to Indian markets (logistics costs, payment infrastructure costs, data costs for Tier 2 users)

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Given a user research summary, a set of 6 proposed solutions, and a team capacity constraint, produce 5 deliverables in sequence: a scoped problem statement, a 2-level MECE tree with all 6 solutions placed, a RICE-scored prioritisation table with full working, a full CIRCLES answer for the top-ranked solution, and 3 measurable success metrics.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at PayEase — a fictional Indian fintech offering UPI payments, bill payments, and a buy-now-pay-later product for online shopping.

Situation:
- 2.4 million users started the KYC verification process in the last 90 days
- Only 38% completed KYC (industry average for similar apps: 61%)
- Drop-off analysis: 54% of incomplete KYC users abandon at Step 3 — Aadhaar OTP verification
- User research (15 user interviews, 200 support tickets): users either do not receive the OTP, receive it late, or do not understand that they have only 3 minutes to enter it before it expires
- Engineering team: 3 engineers, capacity for 4 weeks of work before the quarter ends

Six proposed solutions:

Solution A: Extend OTP expiry from 3 minutes to 10 minutes
Reach: 1,500,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 85% | Effort: 1 week (1 engineer-week)

Solution B: Add real-time OTP status indicator on screen ('OTP sent — check your SMS inbox')
Reach: 1,500,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 1 week (1 engineer-week)

Solution C: Add DigiLocker as alternative verification path for users with Aadhaar linked
Reach: 600,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 65% | Effort: 3 weeks (3 engineer-weeks)

Solution D: Send automated WhatsApp recovery message to users who abandon the OTP step
Reach: 900,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 1.5 weeks (1.5 engineer-weeks)

Solution E: Redesign the full KYC flow with a progress bar and plain-language step-by-step instructions
Reach: 2,400,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 4 weeks (4 engineer-weeks)

Solution F: Add live chat support during KYC for users who get stuck
Reach: 300,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 2 weeks (2 engineer-weeks)

Note: For RICE, use Effort in engineer-weeks (as listed above for each solution).
assessmentTask.outputFormat:
Five clearly labelled sections:

SECTION 1 — PROBLEM STATEMENT: One sentence using the format 'When [user in specific situation] tries to [specific goal], they experience [specific pain], which results in [measurable business impact].'

SECTION 2 — MECE TREE: A 2-level indented tree. Top-level branches must be MECE. Each of the 6 solutions (labelled A-F) must appear under exactly one sub-branch — no solution in two branches.

SECTION 3 — RICE TABLE: Full table with columns: Solution | Reach | Impact | Confidence | Effort (eng-weeks) | RICE Score | Working (arithmetic). Followed by a ranking from highest to lowest RICE score.

SECTION 4 — CIRCLES ANSWER: All 7 steps written out for the top-ranked solution from Section 3. The Summarise step must be a full paragraph.

SECTION 5 — SUCCESS METRICS: Three metrics in the format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].' One per bullet.

realWorldAnchor.example: Zepto's North Star is likely something like 'monthly transacting households' — a measure of recurring purchase behaviour — while CRED's North Star is closer to 'monthly bill payments via CRED' — these are completely different because Zepto is a frequency-of-purchase business and CRED is a monthly-cycle bill payment business, and the North Star metric you pick shapes every downstream feature and team decision.

days.teamContext: North Star and AARRR metrics are most directly relevant to the PM's relationship with the Data/Analytics team — the North Star is the shared language that tells analysts what to instrument, what to report weekly, and what anomalies to flag to the PM without being asked.

tasks.title: North Star Metric: What It Is and How to Find Yours — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky surveys how top product teams at companies like Duolingo, Figma, Notion, and Spotify define and use their North Star metric. The article explains what makes a good North Star (captures value delivered to users AND predicts long-term business health), shows examples of bad North Stars (revenue, DAU without context), and explains why two companies in the same space can have completely different North Stars depending on their business model.
keyTakeaways: A good North Star metric has three properties: it measures the value your product delivers to users (not just internal business health), it is leading (it predicts future revenue rather than trailing it), and the whole team can influence it — a metric only the CEO can move is a vanity metric, not a North Star

The most common North Star mistake is choosing a metric that is easy to measure rather than one that is meaningful — DAU (daily active users) without a quality filter is often gamed; a better North Star specifies what 'active' means in the context of the product's core value (e.g., 'users who complete at least one grocery order per week')

Zepto and CRED have fundamentally different North Stars because their businesses are different: Zepto wins by increasing purchase frequency per household; CRED wins by increasing the value of bills paid per member — mixing these up leads to building the wrong features

tasks.title: AARRR Pirate Metrics: The PM's Guide to Funnel Thinking — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the AARRR framework (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue) as a structured way for PMs to map all product metrics to funnel stages, identify where users drop off, and prioritise which stage to fix first. For Pranav, AARRR is the bridge between a single North Star metric and the full set of 5-7 supporting metrics a PM monitors weekly.
keyTakeaways: AARRR gives every metric a home: acquisition metrics (how did users find us?), activation metrics (did they experience the core value?), retention metrics (do they come back?), referral metrics (do they tell others?), revenue metrics (do we make money?) — if a metric does not fit one of these five buckets, it is probably not worth tracking weekly

The most important and most neglected stage is Activation — it is the bridge between getting a user and keeping them, and most teams under-invest in it because it does not show up in acquisition dashboards or revenue dashboards; it lives in the middle and is invisible unless you specifically measure it

For Indian consumer apps, Retention and Referral look different than Western markets: retention is shaped by cash-back and offers cycles, and referral is heavily driven by WhatsApp sharing rather than in-app invite flows — a PM who copies US-market AARRR benchmarks without adjusting for India will misread their own funnel

tasks.keyword: Zepto North Star metric quick commerce India CRED North Star difference business model 2023 2024
tasks.whyItMatters: Searching for how Indian PMs and analysts describe Zepto's and CRED's core metrics gives Pranav real context for today's assessment — where the spec asks him to define Zepto's North Star, seeing how practitioners in the Indian market think about it is better preparation than reading a generic framework article. Look for product teardowns, PM blog posts, or investor analysis that specifically discusses what Zepto optimises for.
whatToLearn: What metric Zepto's product team is most likely to use as their North Star given their business model — frequency of household grocery orders, not just app opens or GMV, because the business wins when households replace their kirana store habit with Zepto

Why two companies in adjacent spaces (Zepto quick commerce vs CRED fintech) would have North Stars that are structurally different — and why copying a competitor's metric can destroy your own product focus

tasks.promptTitle: SQL intro — read a real query, explain what it returns, extend it
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me build SQL reading fluency — the ability to look at a real query and understand what it returns, without needing to write SQL myself.

Today I studied:
- North Star metric: a single metric that captures the value the product delivers to users and predicts long-term business health
- AARRR funnel: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — every product metric belongs to one of these five stages
- Why Zepto and CRED have completely different North Stars because their business models are different

Real world context: I am an APM at Zepto. My data analyst just shared this SQL query that runs every Monday morning to check the previous week's order performance:

```sql
SELECT
  DATE(created_at)        AS order_date,
  delivery_city           AS city,
  COUNT(order_id)         AS total_orders,
  SUM(order_value_inr)    AS total_revenue_inr,
  ROUND(AVG(order_value_inr), 2) AS avg_order_value_inr
FROM orders
WHERE order_status = 'completed'
  AND created_at >= DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 7 DAY)
GROUP BY DATE(created_at), delivery_city
ORDER BY order_date DESC, total_orders DESC;
```

Your role today: Act as the analyst who wrote this query. Answer my questions directly and honestly. If I misread the query, correct me. If I ask something the query cannot tell me, explain why.

Activity — 3 questions:
Question 1: I will describe in plain English what this query returns — what rows it produces, what each column contains, and what time period it covers. You confirm or correct.
Question 2: I will identify one thing this query CANNOT tell me that I would want to know as a Zepto APM. You confirm or correct.
Question 3: I will write the plain-English instruction I would give to the analyst to ALSO show orders broken down by hour of day (not just by date). You then show me what the SQL change would look like.

After all 3 questions, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — e.g., why a Zepto analyst running this query every Monday morning is specifically watching for city-level anomalies, and what a city-level drop typically signals in Indian quick commerce vs a national drop]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip for Pranav on reading SQL as a PM — what to look at first, what to ignore]

Begin with: 'Good. Look at the query carefully. Start with Question 1: in plain English, tell me what this query returns — what does each row represent, what does each column contain, and what time period does it cover?'

days.theme: Diagnosing metric drops and root cause analysis
days.objective: Build a full RCA tree for a specific dashboard anomaly, identify the most likely root cause from a set of data signals, and list 3 prioritised next data checks with the reasoning behind each.

realWorldAnchor.example: A Monday morning order volume drop at Swiggy is one of the most commonly cited PM interview scenarios in India — it became a reference case because Swiggy PMs reportedly use this exact type of dashboard anomaly during on-call rotations, and the structured diagnosis approach (funnel stage isolation, platform split, geography split, time-of-day split) is a repeatable playbook across Indian consumer internet companies.

days.teamContext: Metric drop diagnosis is the PM's primary interface with the Data team during an incident — the PM's job is to generate and prioritise hypotheses, while the analyst runs the queries. A PM who can specify exactly what data check to run next (rather than asking 'can you check the data?') compresses the diagnosis loop from days to hours.

tasks.title: A Framework for Diagnosing Metric Drops — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: This article lays out a structured step-by-step playbook for diagnosing a product metric drop — covering how to isolate the affected funnel stage, how to split by platform and geography, and how to differentiate between internal (product/engineering) and external (market/competitive) causes. For Pranav, this is the operational version of Week 1's MECE tree: a specific diagnosis playbook rather than a general thinking method.
keyTakeaways: The first question in any metric drop is: which part of the funnel is affected? A drop in orders with flat sessions means the problem is in the conversion funnel (activation or checkout); a drop in both orders and sessions means the problem is upstream (acquisition or a major bug causing users to not even open the app)

The second split is always platform: iOS vs Android vs web. A drop isolated to one platform almost always points to a recent code release, an OS-level change, or a platform-specific infrastructure issue — if both platforms drop together, the cause is product-level or external

Time-of-day and geography splits are the two most underused diagnostic moves in India specifically: Indian metro vs Tier 2 data often moves differently during regional holidays, cricket match evenings, and salary-day cycles — a PM who only looks at national numbers will miss a city-level or time-specific anomaly

tasks.title: Root Cause Analysis for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how to apply the root cause analysis (RCA) method specifically in a product context — using the 5 Whys technique and fishbone diagrams alongside funnel data to move from a symptom ('orders dropped 18%') to a root cause ('Android app version 3.2.1 introduced a payment step regression that broke checkout for 31% of Android users'). The article bridges Day 2's MECE tree thinking with Day 8's metric framework.
keyTakeaways: RCA in product management has a specific structure: start with the symptom (the metric that moved), isolate the affected segment (funnel stage, platform, geography, cohort), form a specific hypothesis, verify it with one targeted data check, then iterate — this is faster than building an exhaustive tree before running any data

The 5 Whys technique is most useful once you have identified the affected segment: 'Orders dropped' → 'Android orders dropped' → 'Android checkout completion dropped' → 'Android payment step has a new error state' → 'The v3.2.1 release changed the payment SDK timeout parameter' — each 'why' narrows the scope and points to a specific fix

The most common RCA mistake in PM interviews is confusing correlation with causation: a Swiggy PM who sees that order drop correlates with rain might conclude rain caused the drop, when actually a delivery partner availability drop (caused by rain) caused the drop — the mechanism matters for the fix

tasks.keyword: Swiggy order drop root cause analysis Monday product analytics diagnosis India PM interview case

Look for PM prep blogs, YouTube walkthroughs, or LinkedIn posts that walk through this exact scenario step by step.
whatToLearn: What the standard diagnosis sequence looks like for this case: funnel isolation first (sessions vs conversion vs checkout), then platform split, then geography split, then time-of-day, then external factors — and why this sequence is not arbitrary but follows the logic of what each split can rule out
What the top hypothesis typically turns out to be in worked examples of this case, and what data check would confirm or reject it within 30 minutes of starting the investigation

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Build a full 2-level RCA tree for a Swiggy dashboard anomaly using 7 data signals, identify the most likely root cause, and list 3 prioritised data checks with the reasoning behind each.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at Swiggy. It is Monday 9am. The weekly dashboard shows the following vs the same Monday last week:

Signal 1: Total orders — down 18% week-on-week
Signal 2: App sessions (opens) — flat (0% change)
Signal 3: Overall conversion rate (sessions to completed orders) — down 14%
Signal 4: Cart abandonment rate — no change (flat)
Signal 5: Drop-off at the payment step — up 22%
Signal 6: Android order volume — down 31%
Signal 7: iOS order volume — flat (0% change)

Additional context:
- Engineering shipped a new version of the Android app (v8.4.1) last Thursday
- No new marketing campaigns are running this week
- No known competitor promotions this week
- Weather is normal in all top cities

Your deliverables:

A. RCA TREE: Build a 2-level MECE tree. Top-level branches must cover all possible causes for the order drop given the 7 signals above. At the second level, expand at least 2 branches into sub-causes. Label where each of the 7 signals provides evidence (i.e., which branch does each signal help rule in or rule out?).

B. ROOT CAUSE HYPOTHESIS: State your single most likely root cause in one specific sentence. Include: what broke, on which platform, caused by what event, affecting which funnel step.

C. THREE NEXT DATA CHECKS: List 3 specific data checks you would run in order of priority. For each: state exactly what you would ask the analyst to query, what result would confirm your hypothesis, and what result would reject it.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Section A: An indented MECE tree with top-level branches, sub-branches, and signal labels at the leaf level (e.g., 'Signal 6 rules this branch in'). Section B: One specific hypothesis sentence. Section C: A numbered list of 3 data checks, each with — Query description (plain English) / Confirms hypothesis if: [result] / Rejects hypothesis if: [result].

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy reportedly runs hundreds of concurrent A/B experiments at any given time across checkout, restaurant ranking, and delivery ETA display — before any checkout flow change ships to all users, it runs as a controlled experiment on a percentage of traffic, with guardrail metrics monitoring for unintended side effects like increased order cancellations or payment failures.

days.teamContext: A/B testing is the PM's primary tool for working with the Data team to validate a decision before committing to it — the PM writes the hypothesis and specifies the metrics, the analyst sets up the instrumentation and calculates sample size, and both review the results together. A PM who cannot specify guardrail metrics forces the analyst to guess what 'winning' means.

tasks.title: A/B Testing: A Complete Guide for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article covers A/B testing from a PM's perspective — how to design a valid experiment, what makes a hypothesis testable, how to choose a primary metric vs guardrail metrics, and why statistical significance alone is not enough to declare a winner. For Pranav, this is the practical reference for writing the A/B test plan in today's assessment.
keyTakeaways: A valid A/B test hypothesis has three parts: the change you are making (the treatment), the metric you expect to move (the primary metric), and the direction and magnitude of the expected change — 'removing the tip prompt will increase checkout completion rate by at least 5%' is a hypothesis; 'removing the tip prompt will improve UX' is not

Guardrail metrics are the metrics you are NOT trying to move but must monitor to ensure the experiment does not cause harm — for a checkout test, guardrail metrics typically include average order value, payment failure rate, and order cancellation rate — if any guardrail moves negatively, the experiment fails even if the primary metric improves

Sample size and duration are calculated before the experiment starts, not after — the minimum detectable effect (the smallest change you would consider business-significant) drives the sample size calculation, and underpowered experiments produce results you cannot trust either way

tasks.title: Experimentation at Scale: How to Build a Culture of Testing — First Round Review
tasks.summary: This First Round Review article covers how product teams build an experimentation culture — not just how to run individual A/B tests, but how to decide which experiments are worth running, how to handle inconclusive results without reverting to opinions, and how to communicate experiment results to stakeholders who want a cleaner answer than 'it depends.' For Pranav, this adds the organisational dimension to the technical framework from the first reading.
keyTakeaways: The biggest waste in A/B testing is running experiments without a clear hypothesis — teams that test everything without prioritising end up with a backlog of inconclusive results and no learning, because they tested features rather than specific mechanisms

Inconclusive results are not failures — they are information: if a well-powered experiment shows no difference, you now know the change does not move the needle, which is itself valuable for deprioritising that direction and focusing elsewhere

Communicating experiment results to non-data stakeholders (design leads, engineering managers, business heads) requires translating statistical significance into business terms: 'at 95% confidence, the new checkout reduces friction for 1.2 million users per month, generating an estimated 40,000 additional completed orders per month' is what a PM should bring to a decision meeting, not a p-value

tasks.keyword: A/B testing product manager checkout flow India Swiggy Zomato experiment design case study 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing how Indian consumer internet companies design and communicate A/B experiments — specifically for checkout flows where conversion rate improvements translate directly to revenue — gives Pranav real anchoring for writing his own test plan in the assessment. Look for PM blog posts, conference talks, or product teardowns that describe a specific experiment, its hypothesis, and what the team learned.
whatToLearn: What primary metrics Indian e-commerce and food delivery PMs use for checkout experiments — checkout completion rate, time-to-complete, or order value are common choices, each with different implications for sample size and duration

How Indian product teams handle A/B test duration given India's day-of-week and festive season traffic patterns — running an experiment that includes a Sunday (high traffic) and misses a Monday (lower traffic) produces a biased result, so test duration decisions in India require knowledge of the product's weekly traffic curve

tasks.promptTitle: Design and stress-test an A/B test plan — Zomato tip prompt removal
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice designing a rigorous A/B test plan for a real product change.

Today I studied:
- A/B test hypothesis structure: change + primary metric + expected direction and magnitude
- Guardrail metrics: metrics you must monitor to ensure the experiment does not cause unintended harm
- Minimum detectable effect and sample size: calculated before the experiment starts, not after
- How to communicate experiment results to non-data stakeholders in business terms

Real world context: I am an APM at Zomato. The current checkout flow has 5 steps: (1) Review cart, (2) Select delivery address, (3) Tip prompt screen (users can add a tip for the delivery partner — default is 0), (4) Select payment method, (5) Confirm and pay. The hypothesis is that the tip prompt screen adds unnecessary friction and removing it will increase checkout completion.

Your role today: Act as a skeptical data scientist reviewing my test plan before I submit it to the engineering team for implementation. Push back on any hypothesis that is not testable, any primary metric that is too broad, any missing guardrail, and any duration estimate that is not justified.

Activity:
Step 1: I write the hypothesis in the required format: 'By [change], we expect [primary metric] to [direction] by [minimum magnitude] because [mechanism].' You critique the hypothesis.
Step 2: I name the primary metric and 3 guardrail metrics. You push back on any that are missing or poorly chosen.
Step 3: I estimate the minimum detectable effect (MDE) and explain why I chose that magnitude. You challenge whether it is realistic and business-significant.
Step 4: I estimate the test duration and explain how I got there. You check whether it accounts for India-specific traffic patterns.

After all 4 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — the tip prompt for delivery partners has a specific emotional and ethical dimension in India that affects how users respond to removing it: delivery partner welfare is a visible public concern in Indian consumer discourse, and a change that removes the tipping option may create negative PR even if it improves conversion — this is a risk factor that a US-market PM might not weight]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav on A/B test design]

Begin with: 'Let's start with the hypothesis. The change you are testing is: removing the tip prompt screen from Zomato checkout. Write the hypothesis in the exact format I specified. Go.'

realWorldAnchor.example: Razorpay's product team reportedly uses funnel analytics to identify exactly where merchants drop off during API integration — a PM reading the Mixpanel or Amplitude funnel can see whether the friction is in documentation, in the first API call, or in the test-to-live transition, and each diagnosis points to a completely different fix.

tasks.title: Product Analytics Tools: What PMs Need to Know — Product School
tasks.summary: This article surveys the analytics tools a PM encounters in a modern product team — Mixpanel, Amplitude, Google Analytics, Looker, and custom dashboards — and explains what each is used for, what its strengths and limitations are, and what questions it can and cannot answer. For Pranav, this is the orientation layer before reading actual tool outputs in today's other tasks.
keyTakeaways: Mixpanel and Amplitude are event-based analytics tools — they track specific user actions (taps, clicks, form submissions) rather than page views, making them better for product funnels than Google Analytics, which is optimised for content and marketing attribution

A PM's primary use of these tools is funnel analysis (where do users drop off?), retention analysis (do users come back after their first session?), and cohort comparison (do users acquired in January behave differently from users acquired in March?) — these three question types account for most of the analytical questions a PM asks weekly

The biggest PM mistake with analytics tools is over-relying on the tool's default dashboard — default dashboards show vanity metrics (total users, total sessions) not product health metrics (activation rate, week-1 retention, funnel completion rate). A PM who has not configured custom dashboards is flying blind

tasks.title: Cohort Analysis: The Most Useful Framework You're Probably Ignoring — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky explains cohort analysis — grouping users by when they joined and tracking how each group behaves over time — as the single most underused PM analytical framework. The article shows how cohort retention tables reveal whether product changes improved retention for new users, whether certain acquisition channels produce better-retaining users, and where in the retention curve the biggest drop-off occurs.
keyTakeaways: A retention curve has three characteristic regions: a sharp drop in the first week (activation friction), a gradual decline through weeks 2-8 (habituation), and a flat tail (the retained core). Improvements in each region require completely different interventions — onboarding changes improve the early drop, engagement features improve the middle, and core value delivery improvements flatten the tail

Comparing cohorts over time is how a PM measures whether product changes are working: if the March cohort shows better week-4 retention than the January cohort, and a significant product change shipped in February, that is evidence (not proof) that the change helped

For Indian consumer apps, cohort retention is heavily affected by India-specific cycles: monthly salary credit dates, festive season spikes, and cricket match schedules all create periodic peaks that can make a cohort look artificially strong or weak if you do not account for the calendar

tasks.keyword: Razorpay merchant analytics product insights funnel drop-off India PM analytics tools 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Razorpay's product team works with a user base of merchants (businesses integrating payment APIs) rather than end consumers — their analytics questions are about merchant activation, integration completion, and recurring payment volume, not about individual purchase funnels. Searching for how Razorpay's PMs think about analytics gives Pranav exposure to B2B product analytics, which is structurally different from B2C and important to understand as a contrast.
whatToLearn: How B2B product analytics (Razorpay's merchant funnel) differs from B2C consumer analytics (Zepto's grocery funnel) — the key difference is that B2B users are slower to activate, have longer retention tails, and lose much higher value per churned user

What the key funnel metrics look like for a payments API product — API key generation, first test transaction, first live transaction, and recurring monthly volume — and how a PM diagnoses a drop at each stage

tasks.promptTitle: SQL parallel — verify whether a plain-English request and a SQL query match
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior data analyst helping me practice the PM skill of verifying whether a plain-English data request and a SQL query actually match — a daily task when working with analysts.

Today I studied:
- Analytics tools: Mixpanel, Amplitude, custom SQL dashboards — what each is used for
- Cohort analysis: grouping users by join date and tracking behaviour over time
- How to read a retention curve and identify where the biggest intervention opportunity is

Real world context: I am an APM at Zepto. I asked my analyst for a specific data pull. She sent back a SQL query. My job is to verify it matches what I asked for before she runs it on production data — because running the wrong query wastes her time and delays my analysis.

MY PLAIN-ENGLISH REQUEST:
'Give me the number of new users who placed their first order in each of the last 6 months, broken down by the city they ordered from. Only include users who placed the order on Android. Sort by month, then by city alphabetically.'

THE SQL QUERY THE ANALYST SENT:
```sql
SELECT
  DATE_FORMAT(first_order_date, '%Y-%m')  AS cohort_month,
  delivery_city                           AS city,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)                 AS new_buyers
FROM orders
WHERE platform = 'android'
  AND first_order_date >= DATE_SUB(CURDATE(), INTERVAL 6 MONTH)
GROUP BY DATE_FORMAT(first_order_date, '%Y-%m'), delivery_city
ORDER BY cohort_month ASC, city ASC;
```

Your role today: Act as the analyst. Answer my verification questions honestly. If I say the query matches when it does not, correct me. If I identify a discrepancy that does not exist, clarify.

Activity — 3 steps:
Step 1: I compare the plain-English request to the SQL line by line and state whether they match. I specifically identify any part of the SQL that does NOT match what I asked for, or any part of my request that is NOT reflected in the SQL.
Step 2: I write the corrected SQL line (just the one line that needs to change) to fix the discrepancy.
Step 3: I identify one thing the SQL assumes that I did not specify in my plain-English request — something the analyst had to decide herself when writing the query.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about why city-level new buyer cohorts matter specifically for Zepto's expansion strategy — a national cohort table hides whether Mumbai retention is strong and Nagpur retention is weak, which is exactly the signal a PM needs when deciding whether to expand dark stores to a new city]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip for Pranav on verifying SQL queries as a PM — what to check first, what to trust the analyst on]

Begin with: 'Good. Go through the SQL line by line. Start with the SELECT clause — does it return exactly the columns I asked for? Then move to the WHERE clause. Tell me if everything matches, and call out any discrepancy you find.'

tasks.promptTitle: Excel/Sheets parallel — read a cohort retention table and extract insights

You are a senior PM helping me practice reading a cohort retention table — a core PM skill for understanding whether product changes are improving user retention over time.

Today I also studied:
- Cohort retention curves: early drop (activation), gradual decline (habituation), flat tail (retained core)
- How cohort comparisons reveal the impact of product changes over time
- India-specific retention patterns: salary cycles, festive seasons, cricket match schedules distort weekly retention

Real world context: I am an APM at CRED. My analyst shared this 6-month cohort retention table showing the percentage of each monthly cohort that is still active (made at least one bill payment) in each subsequent month.

THE COHORT TABLE:

| Cohort | M0 | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan 2024 | 100% | 61% | 44% | 38% | 35% | 33% |
| Feb 2024 | 100% | 58% | 40% | 34% | 31% | — |
| Mar 2024 | 100% | 63% | 47% | 41% | — | — |
| Apr 2024 | 100% | 57% | 39% | — | — | — |
| May 2024 | 100% | 52% | — | — | — | — |
| Jun 2024 | 100% | — | — | — | — | — |

Note: M0 = month of acquisition. M1 = one month later. '—' = data not yet available.

Your role today: Act as the analyst presenting this table. When I extract an insight, ask me: 'So what?' — push me to connect every data observation to a product action, not just a data description. After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — May 2024 cohort's notably lower M1 retention (52% vs 57-63% for other cohorts) may be partially explained by summer seasonality in India: May is exam season and early summer, a period of reduced discretionary spending when users acquired via promotion-heavy campaigns may be lower-intent than usual]

You are an APM at CRED. Your analyst shares the following Mixpanel funnel for the CRED bill payment user journey. This funnel tracks users from app open to completed bill payment over a 30-day window.

FUNNEL DATA (% of users who reach each step, out of all users who opened the app this month):

Step 2 — Bill Payment screen → Credit card selected: 61% reach this step

Your deliverables:

INSIGHTS: Extract exactly 3 specific insights from this funnel. Each insight must: (a) reference specific numbers from the data, (b) say what the number means in plain English, and (c) state what product question it raises.

EXPERIMENTS: Recommend exactly 2 experiments. For each experiment: state the hypothesis (in the format: 'By [change] we expect [metric] to [direction] by at least [magnitude] because [mechanism]'), state which funnel drop motivated this experiment, and state the primary success metric.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: INSIGHTS: A numbered list of 3 items, each with (a) data observation, (b) plain-English meaning, (c) product question it raises. EXPERIMENTS: A numbered list of 2 items, each with hypothesis / funnel motivation / success metric.

- Cohort analysis: comparing user groups by acquisition date to measure retention curve shape
- SQL verification: checking that a plain-English data request and a SQL query match before the query runs

CRED bill payment funnel — 1.2 million monthly active users. Drop from Step 3 to Step 4 increased from 10% to 13% last month with no engineering changes. CRED's core product loop depends on completing this funnel.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Extract 3 specific funnel insights and recommend 2 experiments with hypotheses and success metrics.

[Pranav pastes his 3 insights and 2 experiment recommendations here]

realWorldAnchor.example: Zomato's group ordering feature — where multiple people can add items to a shared cart before checkout — required a PRD that carefully specified the exact user stories for each participant type (the initiator vs the guest), the edge cases (what happens if a guest adds an out-of-stock item?), and the acceptance criteria that engineering used to confirm the feature was production-ready.
