tasks.title: How to Write a Product Requirements Document (PRD) — Product School
tasks.summary: This article walks through the structure and purpose of a PRD — the document that communicates what to build, for whom, and why, in enough detail that an engineer can scope and build it without constant PM involvement. For Pranav, who has consulting exposure to documentation, a PRD is a familiar structured document but with a different audience and purpose — it is optimised for engineering clarity, not stakeholder persuasion.
keyTakeaways: A PRD is not a functional specification — it describes the user's goal and the business requirement, not the technical implementation. Engineers own the technical design; the PM owns the 'what' and 'why', and the engineer owns the 'how'

The most important section of a PRD is 'out of scope' — explicitly listing what will NOT be built in this version prevents scope creep during development and gives engineers permission to say no to late additions without escalating to the PM

Acceptance criteria are the PM's quality gate: they are testable conditions that, when all met, mean the feature is working as intended. Bad acceptance criteria ('the feature should work well') are untestable; good acceptance criteria ('when a user taps Save for Later, the item moves from the active cart to a Saved section within 500ms and a confirmation toast appears') are specific and verifiable

tasks.title: User Stories: The Complete Guide for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how to write user stories — the 'As a [user], I want [goal], so that [benefit]' format — and how to attach acceptance criteria to each story so that engineering can verify completion without ambiguity. The article also covers story splitting (breaking large stories into testable increments) and the difference between user stories (what the user does) and technical tasks (how engineering implements it).
keyTakeaways: A user story is not a feature description — it is a statement of user intent. 'As a daily Zepto user, I want to save items to a list so that I can reorder them quickly tomorrow' is a user story; 'Build a save-for-later button' is a feature request. The format forces the PM to specify who benefits and why, not just what to build

Acceptance criteria complete the user story by defining the boundary conditions: what must be true when the story is done? They should cover the happy path, at least one edge case, and one error state — a story with acceptance criteria only for the happy path will always have unresolved questions during engineering

In Indian product teams, user stories often need an additional dimension for vernacular and low-connectivity edge cases: 'As a user on a 2G connection in a Tier 2 city, I want the cart to load within 3 seconds even without images' is a valid user story that an Indian PM must write explicitly, because it will never appear in a generic user story template

tasks.keyword: product manager PRD template user stories acceptance criteria Indian startup Zepto Swiggy example 2023 2024
tasks.whyItMatters: Searching for real PRD templates and user story examples used by Indian product teams gives Pranav a concrete reference point for what 'good' looks like at the companies he is preparing to join. Look for PM blogs, LinkedIn posts, or product teardowns that share actual PRD sections or user story formats — even partial examples are useful anchors.
whatToLearn: What a typical PRD looks like at a fast-moving Indian consumer internet company — the format is usually leaner than a corporate spec document, favouring a 1-2 page structure with problem statement, user stories, metrics, and out-of-scope items over a 10-page technical specification

What acceptance criteria actually look like when written by experienced PMs at Indian product companies — specifically, how they handle edge cases for low-connectivity users, vernacular content, and payment-related states (pending, failed, refunded) which are more complex in Indian payment infrastructure than in Western markets

tasks.promptTitle: Figma parallel — write 3 structured PM feedback comments on a Zepto cart screen
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice writing structured Figma feedback comments — the format a PM uses to give actionable design feedback that an engineer can implement without further clarification.

Today I studied:
- PRD structure: problem statement, user stories with acceptance criteria, out-of-scope items, edge cases
- User story format: 'As a [user], I want [goal], so that [benefit]' — with acceptance criteria for happy path, edge case, and error state
- How acceptance criteria must be testable, not aspirational

Real world context: I am an APM at Zepto reviewing the cart screen design before it goes to engineering. The designer has shared a wireframe in Figma and asked for my feedback before the handoff.

THE WIREFRAME DESCRIPTION (treat this as if you are looking at the screen):
Zepto Android cart screen. Top of screen: delivery address showing '14, Koramangala 5th Block, Bangalore' with a small pencil icon next to it but no label saying what the icon does. Below the address: a list of 4 items, each showing the item name, quantity (with + and - tappable buttons), and item price. Below the item list: a horizontally scrolling row of 3 'You might also like' product suggestions with an Add button on each. Below that: a grey text field labelled 'Have a promo code?' with a 'Apply' button. At the bottom: a fixed footer bar showing 'Total: ₹487' on the left and a large green 'Place Order' button on the right. There is no delivery fee line item shown anywhere on the screen.

Figma comment format — use EXACTLY this structure for each comment:
[COMMENT N]
Location: [specific screen element or area]
Issue: [what the problem is — describe it as a user would experience it]
Suggestion: [specific, implementable change — one sentence]
Priority: [P1 = blocks launch / P2 = should fix before launch / P3 = nice to have]

Your role today: Act as the designer receiving my feedback. For each comment I write:
- If the location is vague (e.g., 'the bottom area'), ask me to be more specific
- If the issue is an opinion rather than a user problem (e.g., 'this looks cluttered'), ask me to reframe it as a user experience problem
- If the suggestion is not implementable in one sentence, ask me to simplify it

Activity:
I write 3 Figma comments on this cart screen. You review each one before I write the next.

After all 3 comments are reviewed, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — the missing delivery fee line item on the Zepto cart screen is a significant trust issue in the Indian e-commerce context: Indian users, especially in Tier 2 cities, are highly sensitive to hidden charges appearing at checkout — 'surprise' delivery fees at the final payment step are a documented cause of cart abandonment in Indian consumer research]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip for writing PM Figma comments that engineers love vs comments that frustrate them]

Begin with: 'Good. You have the wireframe in front of you. Write your first Figma comment. Use the exact format: [COMMENT 1] / Location / Issue / Suggestion / Priority. Pick the most important problem you see on this screen first.'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write a mini PRD for the Zepto 'Save for Later' feature — including a scoped problem statement, 3 user stories with acceptance criteria, 2 success metrics, a numbered out-of-scope list, and one edge case flagged to engineering.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at Zepto. The feature: a 'Save for Later' option in the cart that lets users move items out of the active cart without deleting them, storing them in a separate 'Saved Items' section they can add back to the cart on a future visit.

Background you have:
- From Day 3's user research: users who buy the same groceries daily find the cart frustrating because they have to re-add items every time. A Save for Later feature would let them maintain a persistent list of staples.
- Cart abandonment rate: 34% of users who add items to cart do not complete the order
- Users who abandon their cart: 41% return within 24 hours and place an order (with or without the same items)
- The feature should work on Android first (iOS to follow in the next sprint)

Write the following sections:

1. PROBLEM STATEMENT — one sentence in the format: 'When [user in specific situation] tries to [goal], they experience [pain], which results in [business impact].'

2. USER STORIES — write exactly 3, each in the format: 'As a [specific user type], I want to [specific action], so that [specific benefit].' Each user story must have acceptance criteria covering: (a) the happy path, (b) one edge case, (c) one error state.

3. SUCCESS METRICS — exactly 2 metrics in the format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].'

4. OUT OF SCOPE — a numbered list of at least 4 items that explicitly will NOT be built in this version.

5. EDGE CASE FOR ENGINEERING — describe one specific edge case this feature creates that engineering needs to handle, in 2-3 sentences. Be specific: what is the scenario, what could go wrong, and what should the product do?
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Five clearly labelled sections. User stories section must show each story's format line followed by an indented list of acceptance criteria labelled (a), (b), (c). Out of scope must be a numbered list. Edge case must be a paragraph of 2-3 sentences.

days.theme: Roadmapping and stakeholder communication
days.objective: Sort 10 features into a Now/Next/Later roadmap with written justification for each placement, and write a 1-page leadership update that communicates what shipped, what the data shows, and what is next — with a different emphasis for engineering vs leadership audiences.

realWorldAnchor.example: Razorpay's product team reportedly communicates roadmap differently to three audiences: to sales they emphasise features that close deals; to engineering they emphasise technical dependencies and sequencing; to leadership they emphasise business outcomes and competitive positioning — the same roadmap, told three different ways, because each audience's question is different.

days.teamContext: Roadmapping and stakeholder communication span all four cross-functional relationships — Engineering needs sequencing and dependency clarity, Design needs feature intent and user context, Data needs success metrics upfront, and Leadership needs business outcome framing. A PM who communicates the same roadmap the same way to all four audiences loses each of them.

tasks.title: How to Build a Product Roadmap — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the purpose and structure of a product roadmap — what it is (a communication tool expressing product direction), what it is not (a project plan with fixed dates), and how to choose between roadmap formats (Now/Next/Later vs quarterly themes vs feature-based). For Pranav, understanding roadmap formats matters because the format chosen signals the company's product maturity — a date-based roadmap in a fast-moving startup is a commitment nobody can keep.
keyTakeaways: A product roadmap is a communication tool, not a commitment document — its job is to align stakeholders on direction and priorities, not to promise delivery dates. PMs who treat their roadmap as a contract end up managing stakeholder expectations instead of building product

Now/Next/Later is the most honest roadmap format for fast-moving teams: 'Now' means actively being built this sprint, 'Next' means the next priority once Now is done, and 'Later' means acknowledged but not scheduled — it is deliberately timeline-free, which prevents the trap of over-committing to future work

The biggest roadmap anti-pattern is feature-stuffing: adding everything to the roadmap to satisfy stakeholders, which signals that nothing is truly prioritised. A roadmap with 4 items in Now, 6 in Next, and 3 in Later is credible; a roadmap with 20 items in Now is not a roadmap, it is a wish list

tasks.title: The Art of the Leadership Update: How PMs Communicate Up — First Round Review
tasks.summary: This First Round Review piece covers how strong PMs communicate with leadership — what information to lead with, how to frame uncertainty without losing credibility, and how to distinguish between a status update (what happened) and a leadership update (what it means and what we are doing about it). For Pranav, this is the writing skill that separates an APM who gets promoted from one who stays invisible.
keyTakeaways: A leadership update has three parts: what happened (specific data, not impressions), what it means (the business implication of that data), and what we are doing next (a clear decision or recommendation, not a list of options). Leaders who receive 'here is what we are exploring' instead of 'here is what we are doing and why' lose confidence in the PM

Leading with data before context is the most common APM communication mistake — starting with 'KYC completion rate improved 12%' without first saying 'the KYC project we launched 2 weeks ago is now showing results' forces the reader to remember context they may not have

Managing up does not mean reporting up — it means giving your manager or leadership team the information they need to make a decision or unblock you, proactively, before they have to ask. A PM who waits to be asked is always one step behind

tasks.keyword: Razorpay product roadmap communication stakeholder engineering sales leadership India PM 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Searching for how Razorpay's product team describes their roadmap communication internally — or how former Razorpay PMs describe it in interviews and blogs — gives Pranav a real reference point for a B2B product company where stakeholder alignment is structurally harder than at a B2C consumer app (because sales, engineering, and leadership all have different urgencies and different definitions of 'done').
whatToLearn: How a B2B product company like Razorpay handles the tension between sales-driven roadmap requests ('the enterprise client needs this feature to sign the contract') and product-led priorities ('this feature serves 200 merchants vs the infrastructure work that serves 200,000') — this tension is more acute in Indian B2B companies where enterprise sales often drives disproportionate revenue

How PMs at Indian payment companies communicate regulatory roadmap items — features mandated by RBI, NPCI, or banking partners — to leadership alongside discretionary product improvements, and how they frame urgency differently for compliance vs growth work

tasks.promptTitle: Excel/Sheets parallel — sort 10 Razorpay features into a Now/Next/Later roadmap
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice building a Now/Next/Later roadmap and justifying each placement — a core PM skill for communicating priorities to engineering, design, and leadership.

Today I studied:
- Product roadmap formats: Now/Next/Later is timeline-free and honest; date-based roadmaps in fast-moving teams create commitment traps
- Roadmap as a communication tool: its job is to align stakeholders on direction, not to promise delivery
- Leadership communication: what happened / what it means / what we are doing next

Real world context: I am an APM at Razorpay. It is the start of Q3. Engineering capacity for the next 3 months: 8 engineers, 2 sprints per month. I need to build the Now/Next/Later roadmap for the merchant dashboard squad and present it to engineering and leadership this week.

Your role today: Act as my PM lead reviewing my roadmap. Push back on any 'Now' placement that seems too ambitious given the team size. Challenge any 'Later' placement that looks like it is avoiding a difficult but important piece of work. Ask me for dependency reasoning — what must ship before something else can start?

Here are the 10 features in the backlog:

1. Auto-reconciliation dashboard — merchants can see all transactions matched against bank statements automatically (effort: high — 6 weeks)
2. Smart retry for failed recurring payments — system automatically retries failed subscription charges at optimal times (effort: medium — 3 weeks)
3. GST-compliant invoice auto-generation — Razorpay generates a GST invoice after every transaction automatically (effort: medium — 3 weeks)
4. Multi-currency support — merchants can accept USD, EUR, GBP (effort: very high — 10 weeks)
5. Payment link expiry notification — sender gets an SMS/email when a payment link they sent is about to expire (effort: low — 1 week)
6. Dark mode for merchant dashboard (effort: low — 1.5 weeks)
7. QR code offline payments — generate a QR code from the dashboard for in-person collection (effort: medium — 4 weeks)
8. Bulk payout scheduling — schedule payouts to multiple vendors in a single batch (effort: high — 5 weeks)
9. WhatsApp payment confirmation to customers — customer gets a WhatsApp message after paying (effort: low — 1 week, pending WhatsApp Business API approval)
10. Analytics API for enterprise merchants — programmatic access to transaction and settlement data (effort: very high — 8 weeks)

Activity:
Step 1: I place all 10 features into Now / Next / Later with a 1-sentence justification for each placement.
Step 2: You push back on at least 2 placements and ask me to defend them.
Step 3: I revise any placements I agree need to change, and hold firm on any I disagree with — explaining why.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — GST-compliant invoice generation and RBI-mandated features occupy a special position in Indian product roadmaps: they are not optional, not prioritised by user value, and not deprioritisable the way product features are — a PM who puts a regulatory compliance feature in 'Later' without checking if there is a compliance deadline will create a serious problem]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav on roadmap justification — what makes a 'Later' justification credible vs evasive]

Begin with: 'Good. You have 8 engineers for 3 months — roughly 24 engineer-weeks of capacity. Now/Next/Later is your format. Place all 10 features and give me one sentence of justification for each. Start.'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write a 1-page leadership update for a PhonePe KYC project — covering what shipped, what the data shows, and what is next — and then rewrite the same update as a 5-bullet engineering team summary with different emphasis.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at PhonePe. Two weeks ago your team shipped a new KYC flow redesign. Here is everything you know:

WHAT SHIPPED:
- New KYC flow with a 4-step progress bar (previously no progress indicator)
- Plain-language instructions at each step in both English and Hindi
- OTP expiry timer now visible on screen (previously hidden)
- Bug fix: address input field was not saving correctly on Android 12 (fixed in this release)

DATA (2 weeks post-launch, compared to 2 weeks pre-launch):
- Overall KYC completion rate: 51% (was 39%) — improvement of 12 percentage points
- Step 1 → Step 2 completion: 89% (was 81%)
- Step 2 → Step 3 completion: 76% (was 71%)
- Step 3 → Step 4 (Aadhaar OTP) completion: 61% (was 58%)
- Step 4 → KYC complete: 94% (was 95%) — slight regression
- Drop-off at Step 3 is still disproportionately high vs the industry benchmark of ~78% for this step
- Support tickets related to KYC: down 34%

WHAT IS NEXT:
- Q3 plan: full rebuild of Step 3 (Aadhaar OTP) — adding an OTP not received flow, alternative verification via DigiLocker, and a retry with different number option
- Estimated impact: if Step 3 completion reaches 78% (industry benchmark), overall KYC completion rate would reach ~62%
- Engineering capacity needed: 4 engineers for 6 weeks

Write two documents:

DOCUMENT 1 — LEADERSHIP UPDATE (1 page, ~300 words):
Audience: CPO and Head of Growth. They care about business impact and what is coming next. They do not need technical detail. Structure: what shipped and what changed / what the data shows and what it means / what we are doing next and why.

DOCUMENT 2 — ENGINEERING TEAM SUMMARY (5 bullets, ~100 words total):
Audience: Engineering lead and 4 engineers on the squad. They care about what worked technically, what the data revealed about the product, and what the next build scope is. Different emphasis from Document 1 — highlight the Step 3 regression, the Step 4 slight regression, and the Q3 rebuild scope.

Today you read it to evaluate yourself: which of the six Week 2 competencies (Fluency with Data, Feature Specification, Product Delivery, Business Outcome Ownership, Product Vision + Roadmapping, Stakeholder Management) are you currently strongest in? Which are you most uncertain about? Use this re-read as a self-diagnostic before the integration case.
keyTakeaways: The four Reforge pillars are not independent — a PM who is strong in Customer Insight but weak in Influencing People cannot ship the products they discover; a PM who is strong in Product Execution but weak in Product Strategy builds the right features for the wrong goals

Week 2's six competencies span three pillars: Fluency with Data and Feature Specification are Product Execution; Business Outcome Ownership is Product Strategy; Stakeholder Management and Managing Up are Influencing People — a balanced Week 2 APM is developing across all three pillars simultaneously

The most common Week 2 gap for APMs is Business Outcome Ownership — APMs who are strong at data analysis and writing specs often struggle to connect their work to the company's financial or strategic outcomes. The OKR framework (used in today's case) is the bridge between feature-level work and business-level outcomes

tasks.promptTitle: Guided warm-up — connect all 5 Week 2 tools to the LoanPe complaint spike before the full case
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me warm up before my Week 2 integration case, by quickly connecting each of Week 2's tools to the same problem scenario.

This week I learned:
- Day 8: North Star metric + AARRR funnel
- Day 9: Metric drop diagnosis + RCA
- Day 10: A/B testing and experimentation
- Day 11: Analytics tools + SQL verification + cohort retention
- Day 12: PRDs, user stories, acceptance criteria, Figma feedback
- Day 13: Roadmapping + stakeholder communication

Real world context: LoanPe is a fictional Indian consumer lending app. This week, complaint volume spiked 4x. 40% of complaints mention 'EMI calculation wrong.' The repayment funnel shows a new drop at the confirmation step. A 30-day retention cohort shows users who saw the repayment screen are retaining at 31% vs 47% for users who did not.

Your role today: Ask me one targeted question for each of the 5 Week 2 tools, to check that I can connect the tool to this specific scenario before I write the full case. One question at a time. Do not move on until I answer.

The 5 questions you will ask me (one at a time):
1. Fluency with Data: 'Given the complaint volume spike and the cohort data, what is the single most important data check you would run first — and why that one before anything else?'
2. Feature Specification: 'If your top hypothesis is confirmed, what is the minimum viable fix — the smallest change to the product that would address the root cause without a full rebuild?'
3. Business Outcome Ownership: 'How would you quantify the business impact of this complaint spike in terms the CPO cares about — not complaint count, but revenue, retention, or LTV?'
4. Product Vision + Roadmapping: 'Where does the fix sit in a Now/Next/Later roadmap — and what does that tell you about how urgent it really is?'
5. Managing Up: 'What is the first sentence of your leadership update about this incident — before you have confirmed the root cause?'

After all 5 questions, tell me which tool I applied most confidently and which one I should slow down on in the full assessment.

Begin with question 1.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Given a complaint spike log, a funnel anomaly, and a retention cohort for a fictional Indian lending app, produce 5 deliverables in sequence: RCA tree, root cause hypothesis, mini PRD for the fix, 3 OKRs for next quarter, and a 5-line leadership update.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at LoanPe — a fictional Indian consumer lending app that offers personal loans and buy-now-pay-later products. Users take a loan, receive the funds, and repay via EMIs through the app.

COMPLAINT LOG — THIS WEEK:
Total new complaints: 1,247 (vs weekly average of ~310 last month — a 4x spike)
Top complaint themes:
- 'EMI calculation wrong' — 498 complaints (40%): 'App shows my EMI as ₹3,420 but when I add up principal + interest it comes to ₹3,390. Where does the extra ₹30 go?' / 'The amount on the repayment screen changes after I tap Confirm. I have a screenshot.'
- 'Payment not reflected' — 311 complaints (25%): EMI paid but app still shows it as due
- 'Cannot see repayment schedule' — 186 complaints (15%): 'How do I see all my remaining EMIs in one place?'
- Other — 252 complaints (20%)

FUNNEL DATA — REPAYMENT FLOW (vs same period last month):
Step 1: Repayment screen opened → Amount confirmed: 81% (was 89%) — drop of 8 points
Step 2: Amount confirmed → Payment method selected: 76% (was 77%) — flat
Step 3: Payment method selected → Payment completed: 91% (was 92%) — flat
Note: Engineering shipped app version 4.1.2 nine days ago. No changelog was shared with the PM team.

COHORT DATA — 30-DAY RETENTION:
Users who visited the repayment screen in the last 30 days: 30-day retention = 31%
Users who did NOT visit the repayment screen in the last 30 days: 30-day retention = 47%
Note: 'Retention' here means the user made at least one app open in the 30-day window after their first repayment.

Your 5 deliverables:

1. RCA TREE: A 2-level MECE tree of all possible causes for the complaint spike. Place each of the top 3 complaint themes (EMI calculation wrong / Payment not reflected / Cannot see repayment schedule) in the correct branch. Label where the funnel data and cohort data provide evidence.

2. ROOT CAUSE HYPOTHESIS: One specific sentence naming what broke, when it broke, which screen it affects, and what the user-visible symptom is.

3. MINI PRD: For the fix to your top hypothesis only. Include: problem statement (4-component format), 2 user stories with acceptance criteria (happy path + 1 edge case each), 2 success metrics, and 2 out-of-scope items.

4. THREE OKRs: For the LoanPe product team next quarter. One Objective, three Key Results. The OKRs must address the trust and repayment experience problems revealed by this incident — not just fix the bug but improve the broader repayment experience.

5. LEADERSHIP UPDATE (5 lines maximum): What happened / What we know so far / What we are doing immediately / What we are doing next quarter / What we need from leadership. Each line: one sentence.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Five clearly labelled sections. RCA tree: indented structure with complaint theme placement labelled. Hypothesis: one sentence. Mini PRD: 4-component problem statement, then 2 user stories each with labelled acceptance criteria (a) happy path (b) edge case, then 2 success metrics, then 2 out-of-scope bullets. OKRs: one Objective statement followed by 3 Key Results formatted as 'KR1: [metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [date]'. Leadership update: 5 numbered lines, one sentence each.

days.theme: Strategy, market sizing, OKRs, and TAM/SAM/SOM
days.objective: Size a market using the top-down method with every assumption stated and a sanity check applied, write 3 OKRs with measurable key results for a specific PM context, and build a TAM/SAM/SOM model using provided assumptions.

realWorldAnchor.example: Zomato's reported evaluation of entering B2B catering — supplying corporate cafeterias and event catering at scale — required a structured market sizing exercise: how large is the Indian B2B food service market, how much of it is currently unorganised, what share could a tech-enabled platform realistically capture, and does the unit economics work at that scale? Publicly available analyst reports and Zomato's investor presentations reference this kind of strategic market analysis.

days.teamContext: Strategy and market sizing are the PM's primary interface with the Leadership and Finance teams — when a PM proposes entering a new market or building a new product line, leadership expects a structured size estimate with stated assumptions, not a confident-sounding number without working.

tasks.title: Market Sizing for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the two market sizing methods used in PM interviews and strategy work — top-down (start with the total population and narrow down) and bottom-up (start with unit economics and scale up). For Pranav, market sizing is both an interview skill and a real PM skill: product decisions about whether to build for a new segment, enter a new city, or launch a new product line all require a structured size estimate.
keyTakeaways: Top-down market sizing starts with the total addressable population and applies filters: total population → relevant demographic → users who need the product → users willing to pay → your realistic market share. Each filter must have a stated assumption — a number without an assumption is a guess, not an estimate

Bottom-up market sizing starts from transaction data: number of units sold × average price × frequency. For a product PM, bottom-up sizing is often more credible because it uses actual behaviour data rather than demographic assumptions — but it requires reliable data, which makes it harder to use for new markets

Sanity-checking a market size estimate means verifying it from a completely different angle: if top-down gives you a ₹50,000 crore market, check it against publicly available data — analyst reports, government statistics, competitor revenue disclosures. If the cross-check is wildly different, your assumptions need revisiting

tasks.title: OKRs for Product Teams: A Practical Guide — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how product teams use OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) to connect quarterly feature work to business outcomes — the Objective sets the direction (qualitative, inspirational), the Key Results define how you will measure success (quantitative, specific, time-bound). For Pranav, OKRs are the bridge between the roadmap (what we are building) and the strategy (why we are building it).
keyTakeaways: An Objective answers 'what are we trying to achieve this quarter?' and must be qualitative and aspirational — 'Make Zepto the default grocery purchase for Bangalore households' is a good Objective; 'Increase orders' is not an Objective, it is a metric

Key Results answer 'how will we know we achieved the Objective?' and must be quantitative, specific, and time-bound — 'Increase week-4 retention from 28% to 38% by end of Q3' is a good Key Result; 'Improve retention' is not. Each OKR should have 2-4 Key Results, not one (one KR can be gamed) and not seven (seven KRs means no prioritisation)

The most common OKR mistake in product teams is writing Key Results that measure output (features shipped, experiments run) rather than outcome (retention improved, conversion increased) — output KRs encourage feature factories; outcome KRs encourage product thinking

tasks.keyword: Zomato B2B catering market entry strategy analysis India food service corporate 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: Zomato's evaluation of the B2B catering and corporate food service market is publicly referenced in analyst notes and investor presentations — searching for analysis of this strategic move gives Pranav a real example of how a consumer internet company evaluates a new market adjacency, including the market size reasoning, the competitive landscape, and the unit economics questions that need answers before committing.
whatToLearn: How Zomato's leadership reportedly thought about the B2B catering opportunity — specifically, how the market size estimate (Indian B2B food service is a large, mostly unorganised market) was weighed against the operational complexity of serving institutional clients vs individual consumers

What the strategic arguments for and against the move were — understanding both sides of a strategic debate is more valuable than knowing the outcome, because the same analytical structure applies to any new market entry question in a PM interview

tasks.promptTitle: Excel/Sheets parallel — build a TAM/SAM/SOM model for Indian quick commerce
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice building a TAM/SAM/SOM market sizing model — a core skill for product strategy presentations and PM interviews.

Today I studied:
- Top-down market sizing: population → relevant demographic → willing to pay → realistic share
- Bottom-up market sizing: units × price × frequency
- Sanity-checking: verifying your estimate from a completely different angle
- OKRs: connecting quarterly feature work to business outcomes

Real world context: I am an APM preparing a market sizing model for Indian quick commerce (10-minute grocery and essentials delivery). My PM lead has asked me to build a TAM/SAM/SOM model to support a strategic recommendation about whether to expand to 15 new Tier 2 cities.

Your role today: Act as the PM lead reviewing my model. Challenge every assumption I state. If I give a number without a source or reasoning, ask 'where does that come from?' If my SAM is suspiciously close to my TAM, ask me to justify the filter.

Here are the assumptions to work with (provided by the strategy team):
- India total population: 1.4 billion
- Urban population (cities with population > 100,000): ~500 million people, ~120 million urban households
- Households with monthly income > ₹40,000 (the approximate quick commerce core user segment): ~30 million households
- Smartphone penetration in this segment: ~95%
- Current quick commerce service availability (cities where dark stores exist today): covers approximately 35 million of the 30M target households
- Average monthly quick commerce spend per active household: ₹1,600
- Current penetration rate of quick commerce among serviceable households: ~22%
- Realistic 3-year market share for a single player (in cities where they operate): ~30-35%

Activity:
Step 1: I define TAM (Total Addressable Market) — all households that could use quick commerce — and calculate the annual revenue figure using the assumptions.
Step 2: I define SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) — households in cities where quick commerce infrastructure exists today.
Step 3: I define SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market) — what a single player can realistically capture in 3 years.
Step 4: I sanity-check my SOM against one public data point (Blinkit, Zepto, or Swiggy Instamart revenue or GMV from any public filing or analyst estimate).

After all 4 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — the ₹40,000 monthly income threshold used for the TAM filter is a rough proxy for the quick commerce user segment, but in India this threshold varies significantly by city: a household earning ₹40,000 in Tier 2 city X has very different purchasing power and delivery willingness than one in Mumbai — Tier 2 expansion changes the segment definition, not just the geography]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific tip on presenting TAM/SAM/SOM to leadership — what they always push back on]

Begin with: 'Start with TAM. Using the assumptions provided, what is your definition of the Total Addressable Market — and what is the annual revenue figure? Show your working step by step.'

tasks.promptTitle: OKR thread — write 3 OKRs for a Zepto APM focused on activation rate
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice writing OKRs that connect product feature work to business outcomes — not output OKRs (features shipped) but outcome OKRs (user behaviour changed).

Today I studied:
- OKR structure: Objective (qualitative, aspirational direction) + Key Results (quantitative, specific, time-bound outcomes)
- The difference between output KRs ('ship the reorder button') and outcome KRs ('increase repeat purchase rate from 34% to 48%')
- How OKRs connect roadmap items to strategy

Real world context: I am an APM at Zepto, focused on the activation squad. 'Activation' for Zepto means: a new user who installs the app and completes their first order within 7 days. Current activation rate: 31% of new installs complete a first order within 7 days. The squad's Q3 focus is improving this rate.

Your role today: Review my 3 OKRs. Push back if the Objective is not aspirational enough, if any Key Result is measuring output rather than outcome, if a Key Result is not time-bound, or if the 3 Key Results together are not sufficient to achieve the Objective.

Activity:
Step 1: I write 1 Objective for the Zepto activation squad this quarter.
Step 2: I write 3 Key Results that would prove we achieved the Objective. For each, I state: what we are measuring, the baseline, the target, and the deadline.
Step 3: You push back on at least one KR and I revise or defend it.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Zepto's activation rate in India is shaped by a specific behavioural barrier that Western quick commerce companies do not face: many first-time Zepto users already have a kirana store relationship they trust — the activation problem is partly a trust problem, not just a friction problem. OKRs that only target funnel metrics will miss the trust dimension]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for writing Key Results that are ambitious but credible]

Begin with: 'Write your Objective first. Remember: it should be qualitative and aspirational — it should make the team excited, not just informed. What is the Zepto activation squad trying to achieve this quarter in one sentence?'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Size the Indian edtech market for working professionals (not students) using the top-down method with every assumption stated, apply a sanity check, and write 3 OKRs for a Zepto APM focused on improving activation rate this quarter.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
PART 1 — MARKET SIZING:
Size the Indian edtech market for working professionals aged 22-45 who are learning new skills for career advancement (exclude K-12, school tuition, and competitive exam prep).

Use the top-down method. You may use these anchor numbers as starting assumptions (adjust if you have better reasoning):
- India workforce (employed adults aged 22-45): approximately 280 million people
- Proportion with internet access and smartphone: approximately 65%
- Proportion in roles where upskilling is relevant and incentivised: approximately 35% of the internet-connected workforce
- Proportion willing to pay for structured online learning (vs free YouTube): approximately 20% of the relevant workforce
- Average annual spend per paying learner: approximately ₹8,000-12,000 per year

Show every step. State every assumption. At the end, apply a sanity check: find one public reference (an analyst report, a company disclosure, or a news article) that gives a different estimate for the same or an adjacent market, and state whether your estimate is consistent with it or not.

PART 2 — OKRs:
You are an APM at Zepto on the activation squad. Current state:
- Activation rate (new installs completing first order within 7 days): 31%
- Primary drop-off: 48% of new installs never even add an item to cart
- Secondary drop-off: of users who add to cart, 41% do not complete checkout
- Top stated reason from user interviews: 'I wanted to check prices but wasn't ready to order yet'

Write 1 Objective and 3 Key Results for Q3. OKR format:
Objective: [one aspirational sentence]
KR1: [metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [end of Q3]
KR2: [metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [end of Q3]
KR3: [metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [end of Q3]
assessmentTask.outputFormat: PART 1: A numbered list showing each step of the top-down calculation with the assumption labelled at each step. Final answer in ₹ crore. Sanity check: one paragraph referencing a public source and stating whether your estimate is consistent. PART 2: One Objective sentence, then KR1/KR2/KR3 each on its own line in the specified format.

days.theme: Indian product landscape deep dive
days.objective: Compare two Indian product companies across 5 strategic dimensions using a structured framework, and make one specific product recommendation for one of them — supported by user, business, and competitive reasoning.

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy, Zomato, and Blinkit operate in overlapping markets but have made fundamentally different bets: Swiggy built horizontal breadth (food + Instamart + Genie), Zomato acquired Blinkit to own the quick commerce vertical outright, and Blinkit (pre-acquisition) focused on supply chain depth in a smaller number of cities — understanding why each company made different choices is a core PM skill for anyone preparing to join the Indian consumer internet space.

days.teamContext: Deep knowledge of the Indian product landscape is the PM's primary input to the Managing Up and Strategic Impact competencies — a PM who cannot articulate how their company is positioned relative to competitors cannot make a credible strategic recommendation to leadership, because leadership is always thinking about competitive context even when feature discussions are tactical.

tasks.title: How to Run a Competitive Analysis as a Product Manager — Product School
tasks.summary: This article covers the structured approach to competitive analysis for PMs — what dimensions to compare (user segment, core value prop, business model, growth lever, product differentiators), how to distinguish between direct competitors (same user, same job) and indirect competitors (same job, different solution), and how to translate competitive insights into product decisions rather than just observation. For Pranav, competitive analysis is both an interview skill and a daily PM tool.
keyTakeaways: Competitive analysis is not a feature comparison table — it is a strategic map that answers: who is each company building for, what job are they solving, how do they make money, and what would it take for a user to switch from them to us (or from us to them)?

The most useful competitive analysis for a PM is not 'they have this feature and we don't' but 'their product makes a fundamentally different bet about user behaviour — here is why, and here is what that means for our roadmap'

In Indian consumer internet, the most important competitive dimension is often unit economics at scale — two companies can have similar features but completely different cost structures, and the one with the better unit economics will win a feature war even if the other has a temporarily better product

tasks.title: The Indian Consumer Internet Landscape: What Every PM Should Know — Inc42
tasks.summary: Inc42's coverage of India's internet economy provides the market context that every PM working in or preparing to join an Indian product company needs — which segments are growing, which are consolidating, what the regulatory environment looks like, and how Indian consumer behaviour differs from Western benchmarks. This is Pranav's reference read for thinking about companies like Swiggy, Zomato, CRED, PhonePe, and Razorpay as parts of a connected ecosystem rather than isolated products.
keyTakeaways: Indian consumer internet is driven by three structural forces that shape every product decision: the scale of the next 500 million internet users coming from Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities, the dominance of UPI as a payment rail that changed the economics of every fintech product, and the regulatory environment (RBI, TRAI, IT Act) that creates both constraints and moats

The lines between Indian consumer internet categories are blurring: Swiggy is a food company that does quick commerce; PhonePe is a payments company that does insurance and wealth management; CRED is a credit card bill payments company that does luxury e-commerce. Every major Indian consumer app is expanding into adjacent categories, which means every PM must understand the competitive threat from unexpected directions

Valuation and funding cycles in Indian consumer internet directly affect product roadmap priorities — when capital is cheap (2020-2021), companies expand features and geographies aggressively; when capital tightens (2022-2023), the focus shifts to unit economics and retention over growth, and PMs need to reorient their roadmap priorities accordingly

tasks.keyword: Swiggy vs Zomato vs Blinkit strategic difference product bet quick commerce food delivery India 2023 2024
tasks.whyItMatters: The Swiggy/Zomato/Blinkit triangle is the most-discussed strategic case study in the Indian product and startup community — understanding the different bets each made (Swiggy's horizontal breadth, Zomato's Blinkit acquisition, Blinkit's supply chain depth) from public sources gives Pranav a living case study in product strategy that he can use in interviews, conversations with hiring managers, and in his own product thinking.
whatToLearn: What the Blinkit acquisition rationale was from Zomato's perspective — how Zomato's management publicly explained why owning quick commerce infrastructure was strategically necessary rather than building it organically, and what that says about how they viewed the long-term competitive dynamics of Indian food and grocery delivery

How Swiggy's Instamart-within-Swiggy approach differs from the Blinkit standalone app approach — and what the product implications are for user acquisition, retention, and cross-sell that a PM would need to consider if working at either company

tasks.promptTitle: Deep-dive competitive analysis — CRED vs PhonePe across 5 strategic dimensions
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice structured competitive analysis across two Indian fintech companies — CRED and PhonePe — as preparation for my assessment.

Today I studied:
- Competitive analysis structure: user segment, core value prop, business model, growth lever, competitive moat and risk
- How Indian consumer internet companies are expanding into adjacent categories
- How funding cycles affect roadmap priorities: growth in cheap-capital periods, unit economics in tight-capital periods

Real world context: I am preparing for an interview with an Indian fintech company. The interviewer is likely to ask me to compare CRED and PhonePe — two companies that operate in adjacent spaces but have completely different product and business strategies.

Your role today: Act as an experienced PM who knows both companies well. When I compare them on a dimension, push back if I am describing features rather than strategy. If I say 'CRED has a rewards catalogue and PhonePe doesn't,' ask: 'So what does that mean strategically for CRED's business model? Why does having a rewards catalogue matter for their P&L?' Push the conversation one level deeper than observation.

The 5 dimensions to compare (one at a time):
1. North Star metric — what does each company optimise for, and why are they different?
2. Primary user segment — who are they building for, and how does that shape every product decision?
3. Core value proposition — what is the single most important thing each product does for its user?
4. Main growth lever — how does each company grow its user base and revenue in a sustainable way?
5. Biggest product risk — what is the one thing that, if it goes wrong, could seriously damage each company's product position?

Activity:
For each dimension, I state my comparison. You push back, confirm, or add nuance. We move through all 5.

After all 5 dimensions, you ask me: 'If you were APM at CRED tomorrow — not CEO, not CPO, just an APM with one squad — what is the single most impactful product change you would push for? Why that change, and how would you build the business case for it?'

After I answer, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]

days.theme: Bharat vs India: Designing for India's Next 400 Million Users
days.objective: Understand the product and strategic decisions that enabled Jio to bring 400 million new users online; map the behavioural differences between urban Indian (India) and small-town/rural Indian (Bharat) users; and redesign a feature for Bharat using structured Figma annotations.

realWorldAnchor.context: Jio's 2016 launch fundamentally changed who uses the internet in India. Their product decisions — free voice calls, ₹149/month unlimited data, Hindi-first UX — were deliberate product bets, not engineering choices. Understanding these decisions is foundational to designing for Bharat. ONDC (Open Network for Digital Commerce) is the government-backed protocol that aims to replicate UPI's success for e-commerce — and its success or failure will be decided by whether product teams can make it accessible to both Bharat merchants and Bharat consumers.

days.parallelSkillThread: Figma (Day 17 of thread): Structured annotation — redesigning an urban feature for Tier 2 users with written justification for every design decision.

tasks.title: Jio's product strategy: how one company changed what the internet means in India
tasks.summary: Jio's launch strategy is a masterclass in market creation: they did not compete with Airtel or Vodafone for existing users — they created an entirely new internet user class. The product decisions that drove this (free voice, subsidised handsets, JioPhone lite UI, Hindi content partnerships) are documented across press coverage, case studies, and product teardowns. This reading is a synthesis of that strategy, focused on what the PM decisions were and why they were made.
keyTakeaways: Jio's North Star was not ARPU — it was user activation at scale, because the long-term bet was data monetisation, not per-user revenue in Year 1
The JioPhone keypad form factor was a deliberate product decision: the target user owned a feature phone and would not upgrade to a smartphone for a telecom product alone
Hindi-first content (Jio Cinema, Jio TV) was a product decision, not a localisation afterthought — the insight was that Bharat users would use mobile internet for video first, not text
Free voice calls eliminated the biggest barrier to switching (call credit management) for users who had never held a postpaid plan
The product constraint Jio optimised for: users with 2-4GB data/day on ₹150/month budgets, using phones with 512MB RAM, in areas with inconsistent connectivity

tasks.title: ONDC: what it is, why it matters, and how it is different from Swiggy/Zomato
tasks.summary: ONDC is a government-backed open protocol for e-commerce that aims to unbundle the buyer app, the seller app, and the logistics layer — similar to how UPI separated the payment rail from the bank app. Understanding ONDC matters for PMs because it changes what is technically possible for small merchants in Tier 2 India who cannot afford Zomato/Swiggy commissions. Whether ONDC succeeds depends almost entirely on whether product teams can make it accessible to both Bharat merchants and Bharat consumers.
keyTakeaways: ONDC separates buyer apps (where users browse) from seller apps (where merchants list) — any buyer app can show any seller's products, eliminating exclusive platform lock-in
The strategic purpose: reduce the 15-30% commission that Zomato/Swiggy/Amazon charge by creating competition between buyer apps and seller apps
ONDC's product challenge: the UX is currently too fragmented because there is no single authoritative buyer app, and onboarding a small merchant still requires technical integration
Bharat relevance: ONDC's success case is the kirana store owner in Nagpur who cannot afford ₹10,000/month for a Zomato listing but can be onboarded via WhatsApp Business
PM implication: if you are building for Bharat merchants, you need to understand ONDC's protocol before deciding whether to build on top of it or alongside it

tasks.keyword: Tier 2 Tier 3 India user behaviour mobile internet first time users product design assumptions
tasks.title: Research Bharat user behaviour and design assumptions
tasks.whyItMatters: Most PMs in Indian startups (including those at Bengaluru-headquartered companies) were themselves urban internet users before they became PMs. This means they have a systematic blind spot: they design products for users who look like themselves. Researching the documented behavioural differences between urban and Bharat users — literacy levels, data costs, phone storage, language preference, payment trust — is the first step to closing that blind spot.
whatToLearn: What percentage of Indian smartphone users primarily use regional language interfaces rather than English?
What is the typical RAM and storage constraint on phones used by Tier 2/3 users, and how does this affect app size limits?
What are the documented trust barriers for first-time digital payments users in non-metro India?
Which Indian apps have successfully crossed the India-Bharat divide (high DAU in both metro and Tier 3), and what product decisions enabled this?
What does publicly available data show about average daily mobile data consumption in rural India vs metro India?

tasks.promptTitle: Figma annotation: Redesign the Zepto cart screen for a Tier 2 Hindi-speaking user
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior product designer and PM coach.

Role: You are coaching me through a structured design annotation exercise. My goal is to take an existing urban-optimised Indian app screen and redesign it for a specific Bharat user persona — not just translate the language, but rethink every design assumption.

Today I studied:
- Jio's product strategy for 400M new internet users and the specific product decisions behind their Bharat expansion
- ONDC's open commerce model and why it matters for Tier 2 merchants
- Documented behavioural differences between urban Indian and Bharat users (literacy, data, language, trust, phone specs)

Real world context:
Zepto's cart screen is optimised for an urban user: English interface, high-resolution product images, complex discount stacking UI, trust signals that assume prior e-commerce experience (Zepto Catch, Pass membership). The user I am redesigning for is:
- Name: Ramesh, 32 years old, Nashik (Tier 2 Maharashtra)
- Phone: Android with 2GB RAM, 16GB storage
- Data: Jio 2GB/day plan
- Language: Hindi primary, basic English reading
- Internet experience: 3 years on smartphone, uses WhatsApp, YouTube, Google Pay
- E-commerce experience: has ordered 2-3 times from Meesho, not from quick-commerce apps
- Trust level: cautious about digital payments above ₹200; prefers cash-on-delivery for first orders

Your role: Walk me through this step by step.

Activity steps:

Step 1 — Assumption audit
List 8 design assumptions that Zepto's current cart screen makes about the user. For each assumption, write: [Assumption] → [Why it fails for Ramesh]

Examples to start with:
- 'User can read English product names quickly' → Ramesh reads Hindi primary; English slows his decision-making
- 'User trusts digital payment at checkout' → Ramesh will abandon if cash-on-delivery is not the default option shown
(Add 6 more)

Step 2 — Redesign decisions
For each of the 8 assumptions, write a specific redesign decision. Format:
[Original assumption] → [Redesign decision] → [Figma annotation text I would write]

The Figma annotation text is what you would actually write as a label on the design file for the engineering team.

Step 3 — Priority ranking
Rank your 8 redesign decisions by impact on Ramesh completing his first order. Justify the top 3 with one sentence each.

Step 4 — One thing you would NOT change
Identify one element of the urban Zepto cart that you would keep unchanged even for Ramesh — and explain why.

Feedback format after I complete the exercise:
WHAT WORKED: [specific design decisions that show genuine Bharat insight vs surface-level localisation]
WHAT MISSED: [assumptions I did not catch, or redesign decisions that are actually urban-lens solutions dressed as Bharat solutions]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about the difference between 'translating an app' and 'designing for a different mental model']
COACHING NOTE: [one thing Pranav should do differently the next time he runs a design annotation exercise]

Begin with Step 1 — the assumption audit.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Take one specific feature from an urban Indian app (not Zepto — choose Swiggy, CRED, PhonePe, or Razorpay). Write the current user story as it exists for an urban user. Then fully rewrite it for a Tier 2 user matching Ramesh's profile. For each rewrite, explicitly list every assumption you changed and why.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Urban Indian apps are designed by PMs who mostly live in Bengaluru, Mumbai, or Delhi and use iPhones or high-end Androids. This means their user stories carry hidden assumptions: 'User can read English', 'User has stable 4G', 'User has used this app category before', 'User trusts in-app payment'. Your task is to make these hidden assumptions visible — and then redesign around them.

Pick ONE feature from one of these apps:
- Swiggy: the 'Reorder' feature (one-tap reorder from order history)
- CRED: the 'Rent Payment' feature
- PhonePe: the 'Send Money' flow (UPI transfer to a new contact)
- Razorpay: the payment link creation flow (for a small merchant)

Step 1 — Current user story (urban lens)
Write the user story in standard format:
As a [urban user type], I want to [action] so that [outcome].
Acceptance criteria: list 4-5 specific acceptance criteria that a PM at this company would have written.

Step 2 — Assumption inventory
Before rewriting, list every assumption embedded in the original user story and its acceptance criteria. Format:
[Assumption #] [The assumption] [Evidence it is an urban-first assumption]
Aim for at least 6 assumptions.

Step 3 — Tier 2 user story rewrite
Rewrite the user story for Ramesh (Nashik, 32, Jio 2GB/day, Hindi primary, 2GB RAM Android, cautious digital payment user, WhatsApp/YouTube/Google Pay only).
As a [specific Tier 2 user], I want to [action] so that [outcome].
Rewrite all 4-5 acceptance criteria to match Ramesh's context.

Step 4 — Assumption change log
For each assumption you changed between Step 1 and Step 3, write:
[Assumption changed] → [What it became] → [Why this matters for Ramesh specifically]
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Four labelled sections: (1) Feature chosen and original urban user story with 4-5 acceptance criteria. (2) Assumption inventory — minimum 6 assumptions, each with evidence it is urban-first. (3) Tier 2 user story rewrite with revised acceptance criteria. (4) Assumption change log — one row per changed assumption. Total length: aim for 500-700 words.

days.theme: Case Practice: Product Design + Estimation + API Basics
days.objective: Build fluency in two real PM interview formats (product design case and estimation case) under time pressure; and develop foundational API literacy — the ability to have an informed conversation with engineering about what is technically feasible and what questions to ask before writing a spec.

realWorldAnchor.context: Product design and estimation questions are the two most common formats in APM interviews at Indian companies. Jio's rural Maharashtra expansion is the context for today's design case — because designing for connectivity-constrained, Hindi-first users is a real product problem every Indian PM team faces. The API basics thread is anchored in Rapido's ride-booking flow: understanding why PM decisions (show estimated fare before booking, support voice-based destination input) are only possible if specific APIs are available is the concrete way to build API intuition.

days.parallelSkillThread: API Basics (Day 18 of thread): What an API is, why PM decisions depend on API availability, and what questions to ask engineering before spec-ing any feature.

tasks.title: APIs for product managers: what every PM needs to know without writing code
tasks.summary: Most APMs can describe a feature in a PRD but cannot have an informed conversation with engineering about what is technically feasible. The most common gap is API literacy: not knowing what an API is, what it costs to call one, or what questions to ask engineering before assuming a feature is simple. This reading covers the fundamentals: what REST APIs are, what request/response means, what rate limits and latency mean for product decisions, and the specific questions PMs should ask before spec-ing any feature that depends on an external API.
keyTakeaways: An API is a defined contract between two systems: 'if you send me X, I will send you back Y' — PMs need to understand this contract because it defines what is possible, at what cost, and at what speed

Every API call has latency (time to respond) and cost (per-call fees for paid APIs) — PM decisions like 'show real-time fare estimate as the user types' require understanding if the maps API can handle 10 calls per second or costs ₹0.50 per call

Rate limits matter for product design: if WhatsApp's API only allows 1,000 messages per day on a free tier, your 'instant notification' feature is actually a batch notification that might arrive hours late

Key questions PMs should ask engineering before spec-ing a feature: (1) Does this require a new API integration or can we use an existing one? (2) What is the P95 latency of this API? (3) Are there rate limits that affect how often we can call it? (4) What is the fallback state if the API is down?

Rapido's fare estimate feature requires a maps API (route calculation), a demand/surge API (driver availability), and a payment API (UPI link generation) — a PM who does not know these exist cannot spec the feature accurately

tasks.keyword: product manager API knowledge REST API what questions to ask engineering PM technical skills
tasks.title: Research what API literacy looks like in practice for PMs
tasks.whyItMatters: API knowledge is consistently listed in APM job descriptions at Indian companies as 'preferred technical skills'. The actual expectation is not that you can write API code, but that you can read API documentation, understand what a response payload means, and know what to ask engineering before writing a spec. This search helps Pranav calibrate what the bar actually is.
whatToLearn: What does a REST API request and response look like in plain English (not code)?
What is the difference between a GET request and a POST request in terms of what a PM should care about?
What is a webhook and why does it matter for product features like 'notify user when payment is confirmed'?
What questions should a PM ask in sprint planning when engineering says 'this feature requires a new API integration'?
What are the most common APIs used by Indian fintech and consumer apps (Razorpay, Google Maps, Twilio, Firebase push notifications)?

tasks.promptTitle: API basics: map the APIs behind a Rapido booking feature and write the questions a PM should ask
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior technical PM and APM coach.

Role: You are helping me build API literacy by walking me through a real product feature and mapping its API dependencies.

Today I studied:
- What REST APIs are and what request/response means in product terms
- Why API latency, rate limits, and cost affect PM decisions
- The specific questions PMs should ask engineering before spec-ing a feature

Real world context:
Rapido is India's bike taxi and auto-rickshaw booking app. One of their product features is: 'Show the user a fare estimate within 2 seconds of entering the destination, before they confirm the booking.'

This seems like a simple product requirement. But it depends on multiple APIs being available, fast, and reliable.

Your role: Walk me through the API mapping exercise step by step.

Activity steps:

Step 1 — API identification
For the feature 'Show fare estimate within 2 seconds of entering destination', list every external API or internal service that needs to be called. For each one:
[API/Service name] → [What it provides] → [Why removing it breaks the feature]

Step 2 — PM questions
For each API/service identified in Step 1, write the 2 most important questions a PM should ask engineering before spec-ing this feature. Format:
[API/Service] → [Question 1] → [Question 2]

Step 3 — Edge case identification
For the same feature, identify 3 edge cases that a PM should add to the acceptance criteria specifically because of API dependencies. Format:
[Edge case] → [Which API dependency causes it] → [How the PM should spec the fallback behaviour]

Is there a version of this feature that requires fewer API calls? Describe one simpler version and explain what product quality trade-off it makes.

Feedback format:
WHAT WORKED: [specific API reasoning that shows real technical intuition]
WHAT MISSED: [APIs or questions I did not identify]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about API reliability in the Indian infrastructure context — e.g., how 2G/3G fallback matters for Rapido's Tier 2 cities]
COACHING NOTE: [one habit Pranav should build when reviewing engineering specs going forward]

Begin with Step 1.

- [things Pranav missed entirely — e.g., in Case 1: the customer's side of the transaction (if the customer does not have a smartphone, how do they authorise the UPI payment? — this is the actual product problem, not just the merchant's side); ONDC compatibility as a strategic consideration (from Day 17); in Case 2: the utilisation rate variable is the key insight — even if 700K autos are registered, only 60-70% may be active on a given weekday, and within that active fleet, not all are carrying passengers at the same moment]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about how Indian APM interviews specifically differ from US PM interview prep advice — e.g., Indian interviewers often value India-specific constraints being called out (JioPhone spec, ONDC compatibility, RTO registration data for estimation) over generic frameworks applied generically; if Pranav's CIRCLES answer could have been written by someone who has never been to India, that is a missed opportunity]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: Case 1: [X/10] | Case 2: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Fluency with Data — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.theme: Influencing Without Authority: Stakeholder Management and Managing Up
days.objective: Build the Influencing People competencies that separate good PMs from great ones: map stakeholders accurately, communicate decisions in the language each audience needs, and write the kind of managing-up memo that gets a deprioritised feature reconsidered rather than abandoned.

realWorldAnchor.context: Zepto operates at extreme speed — 10-minute delivery means engineering, operations, and product are in constant tension. A Zepto APM does not have the luxury of long alignment cycles. They need to communicate priorities to a founder-level leadership team that moves fast, to an engineering squad that has more than enough on their plate, and to a data team that is shared across squads. Understanding how PMs at high-velocity Indian startups build influence — without formal authority — is the practical content behind the Stakeholder Management and Managing Up competencies.

tasks.title: The PM stakeholder management playbook: mapping, aligning, and communicating across functions
tasks.summary: Stakeholder management is not a soft skill — it is a structured practice. The best PMs maintain a stakeholder map that tracks: who has decision authority, who has influence without authority, who will be impacted by the change, and who needs to be informed after the fact. This reading covers how to build that map for a product squad at a fast-moving company, and how to tailor communication to each stakeholder type: founders want business outcomes, engineering leads want clear scope and stable requirements, designers want user problem framing, data analysts want the metric definition upfront.
keyTakeaways: A stakeholder map has four zones: Decide (has veto power over the decision), Advise (input shapes the decision but does not own it), Execute (builds or operates the output), Inform (needs to know after the decision is made — not before)
The most common APM failure in stakeholder management: treating all stakeholders as 'Decide' and trying to get full consensus before moving forward, which paralyses decision-making

Engineering stakeholder communication requires translating product goals into technical scope: not 'we want users to reorder faster' but 'we want to reduce the taps-to-confirm-reorder from 4 to 2, without changing the cart review screen'
Founder communication at Indian startups is often oral and fast — a 10-slide deck is not always the right format; a two-paragraph Slack update with one number and one ask is often more effective

The RACI model (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) is a useful starting framework but breaks down for fast-moving squads; the practical version is: 'who can say yes, who can say no, who do I tell after'

tasks.title: Managing up as a PM: how to communicate with leadership when you are not the decision-maker
tasks.summary: Managing up is one of the most under-taught PM skills and one of the most important. It is not about being political — it is about giving your manager and leadership the information they need to make good decisions on your behalf. The core skill is translating your ground-level product knowledge into the language of business outcomes that leadership cares about. This reading covers: what managing up actually means, how to write a managing-up document, and the most common ways APMs fail at it (too much detail, wrong metric, no ask).
keyTakeaways: Managing up is not about approval-seeking — it is about keeping leadership informed enough to unblock you, advocate for your work, and make resource decisions in your favour

The structure of a strong managing-up update: (1) Where we are (current status against the metric that leadership cares about), (2) What changed since last week (the one key development), (3) What I need from you (specific and time-bound ask, not 'your thoughts')
The most common APM managing-up mistake: reporting activity ('we shipped the feature') instead of outcomes ('DAU from the reorder flow is up 12% week-on-week since the feature launched')

When your feature gets deprioritised by leadership, managing up is not about arguing — it is about making the opportunity cost of the deprioritisation visible: 'if we do not ship this by end of Q2, we miss the pre-Diwali traffic window which drove 34% of last year's GMV growth'

At Indian startups with founder-led culture, managing up also means managing across — founders often care about your opinions on things outside your squad scope; having a view (and being willing to share it) builds credibility faster than perfect execution in your own lane

tasks.keyword: Indian startup product review meeting PM stakeholder alignment sprint review founder communication
tasks.title: Research how Indian startup PM teams run product reviews and stakeholder alignment
tasks.whyItMatters: Most PM stakeholder management content is written for US tech companies with structured product review processes (Amazon 6-pager, Google PRD review). Indian startups — especially at Series A and B — operate differently: the founder is often in the product review, decisions happen on WhatsApp, and the 'stakeholder map' is sometimes two people. Understanding how Indian PMs actually navigate this is essential context for Pranav.
whatToLearn: How do Indian startup PMs typically run weekly product reviews — what format, what attendees, what decisions get made?
What is the role of the founding team in product decisions at Indian Series A/B startups, and how does this affect the PM's authority?
How do PMs at companies like Zepto, Razorpay, or CRED communicate product strategy decisions to their engineering leads?
What does 'influence without authority' look like in a flat-hierarchy Indian startup vs a more structured organisation like Flipkart or PhonePe?
Are there published case studies or interviews with Indian PMs about how they navigate stakeholder conflict?

tasks.promptTitle: Write a stakeholder communication package for a deprioritised feature at Zepto
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM and communication coach.

Role: You are helping me write a complete stakeholder communication package for a real product scenario at a fast-moving Indian startup.

Today I studied:
- How to build a stakeholder map: Decide / Advise / Execute / Inform zones
- The RACI model and its practical version for fast-moving squads
- Managing up: how to communicate with leadership without over-explaining or under-informing
- How Indian startup PM teams run product reviews

Scenario:
You are an APM at Zepto. Your squad has been working on a feature: 'Reorder basket' — a one-tap reorder of a user's most recent cart, shown on the app home screen during the evening peak window (6-9pm). You shipped an MVP of this feature 3 weeks ago and have early data:
- Users who see the reorder basket convert at 31% vs 18% for users who browse manually (same time window)
- The feature currently shows for only 40% of users (the 40% who had an order in the last 14 days)
- Engineering estimates 3 sprints to expand coverage to 85% of users and add personalisation (show items from the most-ordered category, not just the last cart)

Lastly week, your PM lead told you: 'The reorder basket is on hold — engineering is being pulled onto the new Zepto Catch subscription flow for the next 6 weeks.'

Your task: Write three stakeholder communications.

Communication 1 — Managing up to your PM lead
A Slack message (max 150 words) that acknowledges the decision, makes the opportunity cost visible with the data you have, and asks for a specific commitment (not 'can we revisit this' but a specific ask).

Communication 2 — Informing engineering
A message to your engineering lead (max 100 words) that tells them the feature is on hold, what to do with the work-in-progress, and what the clear trigger is to restart.

Communication 3 — Stakeholder map
Draw the stakeholder map for this feature using the four zones (Decide / Advise / Execute / Inform). For each zone, list the specific roles at Zepto and one sentence on how you would communicate with them about this hold decision.

Feedback format:
WHAT WORKED: [specific communication decisions that show PM maturity — e.g., using the data correctly, being specific with the ask, not over-explaining]
WHAT MISSED: [communication mistakes — e.g., was the managing-up message reporting activity instead of outcomes? Was the ask vague? Was the engineering message demotivating rather than clarifying?]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about communication norms at founder-led Indian startups specifically — e.g., how the tone and format of a Slack message to a founder-PM is different from a message to a hired CPO]
COACHING NOTE: [one communication habit Pranav should build before his first APM role]

Begin by writing Communication 1 as a model, then coach me to write Communications 2 and 3.
