assessmentTask.taskDescription: You are APM at a mid-size Indian fintech. Your top feature — a bill payment reminder that has shown 22% reduction in late fees for users who receive it — has been cut from next quarter's roadmap because leadership wants to focus all engineering on a new 'credit score simulator' feature. Write the full managing-up package: a one-paragraph situation summary, a one-paragraph opportunity cost argument using the data, and a specific one-sentence ask with a deadline.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Managing up is a skill you will use on Day 1 of your first APM role. This assessment asks you to do it in a real scenario — not with a rubric, but with actual data.

The scenario:
- Product: a bill payment reminder feature on a fintech app (similar to CRED's bill reminders)
- Your data: users who received at least one reminder paid their bill on time at a rate of 78%, vs 56% for users who did not receive a reminder — a 22-percentage-point difference in on-time payment rate
- Business relevance: late fees generate short-term revenue but increase churn — the company's current North Star is 90-day retention, and on-time payment is the single strongest predictor of 90-day retention in the company's own cohort data
- The leadership decision: the credit score simulator feature is being prioritised because it is 'more differentiated' and 'will drive press coverage'
- What you are NOT allowed to do: complain, argue that leadership is wrong, or ask for a 'discussion'
- What you ARE trying to do: give leadership the information they need to make a better decision, and ask for something specific

Write three sections:

Section 1 — Situation (max 60 words)
What is the current status of the reminder feature and why does it matter? No opinions — only facts and numbers.

Section 2 — Opportunity cost (max 100 words)
If the reminder feature stays off-roadmap for the quarter, what specifically will the business not achieve? Connect this to the North Star metric (90-day retention) using the data you have.

Section 3 — The ask (max 30 words)
One specific, time-bound ask — not 'can we discuss', not 'I'd like to revisit' — but an actual commitment you are requesting, with a specific deadline.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Three labelled sections: Situation (≤60 words) / Opportunity Cost (≤100 words) / The Ask (≤30 words). Word limits are enforced — managing up is a discipline of brevity. Total: ≤190 words.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my Day 19 work on stakeholder management and managing up.

I studied today:
- Stakeholder mapping: Decide / Advise / Execute / Inform zones
- Managing up: communicating with leadership in the language of business outcomes
- How Indian startup PMs navigate product reviews and founder communication
- Writing stakeholder communications for a deprioritised feature at Zepto

Real world context I should apply:
I am APM at a fintech. My bill payment reminder feature has been cut. My data shows a 22-point on-time payment rate improvement for users who receive reminders, and on-time payment is the strongest predictor of 90-day retention (our current North Star). Leadership has deprioritised my feature in favour of a credit score simulator.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Write a three-section managing-up package (Situation / Opportunity Cost / The Ask) within strict word limits.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his three sections here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets — check: does the Situation section contain only facts and numbers, no opinions? Does the Opportunity Cost section connect the data to the North Star metric, not just to user experience? Is the Ask specific with a deadline, or is it a vague request for consideration?]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets — flag if: Situation contains opinions or framing ('unfortunately' / 'I believe') rather than facts; Opportunity Cost makes the business case but does not quantify what is at stake in the North Star metric specifically; the Ask is vague ('let's discuss'), uses passive voice ('it would be great if'), or lacks a deadline]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things Pranav did not consider — e.g., acknowledging the leadership decision before making the counter-argument (not acknowledging the credit score simulator decision makes the message sound adversarial); offering a path that does not require engineering (e.g., can the reminder run on existing infrastructure at lower cost?); noting that the credit score simulator and the reminder feature are not necessarily competing for the same users — they may address different jobs-to-be-done]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about managing up in a founder-led Indian fintech specifically — e.g., Indian founders often make product decisions based on competitive positioning and press visibility rather than retention data; a managing-up message that only uses internal data without acknowledging the competitive narrative will not land as well as one that connects both]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Managing Up — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Stakeholder Management — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.theme: Product Quality, Launch Readiness, and the PM's Role in Shipping
days.objective: Understand what PMs own (and do not own) in product quality; build a launch readiness checklist that covers user experience, data instrumentation, and rollback criteria; and write a launch plan for a real feature that an engineering team could actually use to ship.

realWorldAnchor.context: Razorpay processes payments for over 8 million businesses in India — a product quality failure is not a bad user experience, it is lost revenue for every merchant whose payment fails. Their engineering and product culture is documented in public engineering blogs, conference talks, and press coverage. Understanding how a payments company thinks about product quality — reliability, observability, graceful degradation — gives Pranav a model for the Product Quality competency that is more rigorous than the typical consumer app framing.

tasks.title: Product quality: what PMs own and what they delegate
tasks.summary: Product quality is one of the most misunderstood PM competencies because it is often confused with QA testing (which is engineering's job). A PM's role in product quality is upstream: defining what 'quality' means for the specific user and use case, setting measurable quality criteria before development begins, instrumenting the right metrics to detect quality degradation after launch, and making the call on whether quality is good enough to ship. This reading defines each of these responsibilities and gives a framework for thinking about quality trade-offs under time pressure.
keyTakeaways: PMs do not test software — they define what 'acceptable quality' means for the user, in measurable terms, before engineering writes a single line of code

Quality criteria belong in the acceptance criteria of every user story: not just 'feature works as intended' but 'feature loads within 2 seconds on a 4G connection in Tier 2 cities, error state shows a readable Hindi message, and the user can retry without re-entering their data'

Observability is a PM responsibility: if you cannot measure whether quality has degraded after launch, you will learn about quality problems from user complaints rather than dashboards — which means you learn late and after damage is done

The quality vs speed trade-off at Indian startups: moving fast is a competitive advantage, but launching with poor quality in payments, healthcare, or lending has regulatory and trust consequences that slow the company down more than a delayed launch would have

Rollback criteria are a PM deliverable: before every launch, the PM should define the specific metric threshold that triggers a rollback — e.g., 'if payment success rate drops below 94% in the 2 hours after launch, we roll back immediately'

tasks.title: Product launch: how to ship a feature and make it land
tasks.summary: Shipping a feature is not the same as launching it. A feature is shipped when engineering merges the code. A feature lands when users discover it, use it, and get value from it. PMs who confuse these two things will consistently under-invest in go-to-market, instrumentation, and post-launch learning. This reading covers the three phases of a product launch: pre-launch preparation (readiness criteria, instrumentation, rollout plan), launch execution (staged rollout, monitoring, comms), and post-launch learning (success metrics, retrospective, follow-on decisions).
keyTakeaways: Pre-launch checklist minimum viable items: (1) Success metric defined and baseline measured, (2) Tracking events instrumented and verified in staging, (3) Rollback criteria agreed with engineering, (4) Staged rollout plan (1% → 10% → 50% → 100%), (5) User-facing copy reviewed in all supported languages

Staged rollout is a PM tool, not an engineering tool — the decision of when to move from 10% to 50% rollout should be driven by the PM's review of quality and success metrics, not by an automatic timer

Post-launch learning requires a structured 'week 1 review': what did the success metric actually do? What is the biggest surprise (positive or negative)? What is the one decision this data drives for the next sprint?

At Indian companies, launch communications often span internal (Slack to the full company), customer-facing (push notification or email), and press (for major features) — each requires different framing and timing

The biggest APM launch mistake: declaring success based on feature adoption rate ('40% of users tried the feature') without checking whether the feature is driving the intended outcome ('did users who tried the feature retain at a higher rate than users who did not?')

tasks.keyword: product launch checklist PM readiness criteria rollback plan staged rollout instrumentation
tasks.title: Research production-ready launch checklists and rollout practices
tasks.whyItMatters: Every APM will ship their first feature within their first 90 days. Having a personal launch checklist — not a generic template but one that reflects the specific quality standards of Indian consumer and fintech products — is one of the most practical tools Pranav can build from this programme.
whatToLearn: What does a production-ready launch checklist look like for a consumer app feature in India?
What specific tracking events should a PM instrument before launching a new feature (e.g., feature_viewed, feature_engaged, feature_completed, error_state_shown)?
What is a 'canary release' or 'staged rollout' and how does a PM decide when to advance each stage?
What rollback criteria do payments companies like Razorpay or Cashfree set before a launch, and how are they different from consumer app rollback criteria?
What does a post-launch retrospective template look like for a PM — what questions should it answer and who should attend?

tasks.promptTitle: Write a complete launch plan for a Razorpay payment link feature
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM and launch readiness coach.

Role: You are helping me build my first complete product launch plan — one that an engineering team could actually use to ship safely.

Today I studied:
- What PMs own in product quality: quality criteria, observability, rollback thresholds
- The three phases of product launch: pre-launch, launch execution, post-launch learning
- Staged rollout, instrumentation requirements, and post-launch retrospective structure

Scenario:
You are an APM at Razorpay on the Payments Links squad. The feature you are launching: 'Instant Payment Link via WhatsApp' — a merchant can generate a payment link directly inside Razorpay's dashboard and share it to a customer's WhatsApp number in one click, without leaving the Razorpay interface. The payment link opens in the customer's WhatsApp, clicking it opens the Razorpay payment page in the browser.

Tech dependencies: Razorpay dashboard (existing), WhatsApp Business API (Twilio), Razorpay payment page (existing). Target users: small merchants (kirana stores, tutors, freelancers) who currently share payment links manually via copy-paste.

Your task: Build the complete launch plan across three phases.

Phase 1 — Pre-launch readiness checklist
For each of the following areas, write 2-3 specific checklist items (not generic statements — specific, verifiable items):
(a) Success metric and baseline
(b) Tracking events to instrument
(c) Quality criteria in acceptance criteria format
(d) Rollback threshold
(e) Staged rollout plan with percentage milestones and go/no-go criteria

Phase 2 — Launch day monitoring plan
Write the monitoring plan for the first 4 hours after launch at 1% rollout. What are you watching, at what frequency, and what triggers a rollback call?

Phase 3 — Week 1 review template
Write the 5 questions your week-1 review will answer, with the specific data source for each answer.

Feedback format:
WHAT WORKED: [specific launch plan decisions that show PM maturity — e.g., rollback criteria that are specific and measurable, tracking events that are named specifically, go/no-go criteria that are not just 'no bugs']
WHAT MISSED: [gaps in the launch plan — e.g., missing WhatsApp API-specific failure modes, no instrumentation for the customer side of the flow (only merchant), no differentiation between Tier 1 and Tier 2 merchant quality expectations]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about product quality standards in Indian payments specifically — e.g., RBI guidelines require specific error handling and audit logging for payment failures, which means rollback criteria at a payments company have regulatory as well as product dimensions]
COACHING NOTE: [one launch planning habit Pranav should build before his first APM role]

Begin with Phase 1.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write the quality criteria and rollback plan for one feature at a company of your choice (from the companies covered in this programme). The feature must be real (publicly documented or logically derivable from what the company does). Your output must be specific enough that an engineering lead could read it and know exactly when to ship and exactly when to roll back.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Product quality is not a feeling — it is a measurable standard set before development begins and monitored continuously after launch. This assessment asks you to write quality criteria with the specificity and measurability that a real APM would need on their first day.

Pick one feature from one company in this programme:
- Swiggy: the 'Instamart dark store availability' indicator on the home screen
- Zepto: the 'Reorder basket' on the home screen during evening peak (6-9pm)
- Razorpay: the 'Instant Payment Link via WhatsApp' feature (from today's activity)
- PhonePe: the 'UPI Lite' offline payment flow
- CRED: the 'Bill payment auto-debit' setup flow

Section 1 — Quality criteria (acceptance criteria format)
Write 5 quality criteria for your chosen feature. Each must:
- Be measurable (not 'works correctly' but 'payment success rate ≥ 96% in P95 latency')
- Cover at least one user-facing quality dimension (speed, clarity, error handling) and one system-level dimension (reliability, data correctness, fallback behaviour)
- Be testable before launch — i.e., a QA engineer reading this criteria should be able to verify it without interpreting it

Section 2 — Rollback plan
Write the rollback plan with three components:
(a) Trigger threshold: the specific metric value and observation window that triggers a rollback decision (e.g., 'if X drops below Y% within Z hours of launch')
(b) Rollback procedure: what happens when the trigger is hit — who makes the call, what gets turned off, what user communication goes out
(c) Recovery criteria: what must be true before the feature can be re-launched after a rollback
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Two labelled sections: (1) Quality Criteria — 5 numbered criteria, each ≤40 words, each measurable and testable. (2) Rollback Plan — three labelled sub-sections (Trigger Threshold / Rollback Procedure / Recovery Criteria), each ≤60 words. Total: aim for 300-400 words.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my Day 20 work on product quality and launch readiness.

I studied today:
- What PMs own in product quality: defining measurable quality criteria, observability, and rollback thresholds
- The three phases of product launch: pre-launch readiness, launch execution, post-launch learning
- Staged rollout, tracking instrumentation, and post-launch retrospective structure
- How to write a launch plan for a real Indian product feature (Razorpay payment link)

Real world context I should apply:
Quality criteria and rollback plans are not bureaucratic documents — they are the PM's way of making the implicit standard explicit. The test of a good quality criterion is: can a QA engineer run a test against it without asking me to clarify? The test of a good rollback plan is: if I am offline and the metric hits the trigger threshold, can the engineering lead make the rollback call without calling me?

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Write 5 quality criteria in acceptance criteria format and a three-part rollback plan for one real feature at one company from this programme.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his quality criteria and rollback plan here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets — check: are the quality criteria actually measurable? Do they cover both user-facing and system-level dimensions? Is the rollback trigger specific (number + time window) or vague ('if there are problems')? Is the recovery criteria different from the original quality criteria, or is it just a restatement of them?]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets — flag if: quality criteria use subjective language ('fast', 'smooth', 'no errors') rather than specific thresholds; rollback trigger does not specify an observation window (saying 'if success rate drops below 95%' without saying 'within 2 hours of launch' is not actionable); rollback procedure does not name who makes the call; recovery criteria are too vague to be verifiable]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things Pranav did not consider — e.g., the difference between a technical rollback (turning off a feature flag) and a data rollback (what happens to transactions that already completed during the degraded state?); the user communication dimension of a rollback (what does the user see or receive if the feature is turned off while they are mid-flow?); the India-specific reliability consideration — 2G/3G fallback states in Tier 2 cities need their own quality criteria separate from the primary 4G state]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about quality standards in the specific domain Pranav chose — payments, quick-commerce, or credit — where Indian regulatory or operational constraints (RBI, TRAI, low-connectivity infrastructure) make the quality bar different from a US consumer app]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Product Quality — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Product Delivery — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.objective: Synthesise all 21 days of learning into a single integrated PM output — a complete product case that requires strategy, execution, user insight, and stakeholder thinking simultaneously — and close the OKR thread by writing the OKRs you would actually pursue in your first 90 days as an APM.

realWorldAnchor.context: Blinkit (formerly Grofers, acquired by Zomato in 2022) is India's market leader in quick-commerce — 10-minute grocery and essential delivery through a network of dark stores. As of 2024, Blinkit operates primarily in metro and Tier 1 cities. Expanding to Tier 2 cities is their documented next growth frontier. This expansion is not a simple geographic rollout — it requires rethinking the dark store model for lower order density, adjusting the product catalogue for different household basket patterns, redesigning the app for users similar to Ramesh (from Day 17), and making the unit economics work at lower average order values. It is the ideal comprehensive case for this final integration day.

days.parallelSkillThread: OKRs (Day 21 — closes the thread from Day 15): Write the final, polished OKR set for your first 90 days as an APM, stress-tested against everything learned in this programme.

tasks.title: The first 90 days as a PM: what to learn, what to ship, and what to resist
tasks.summary: The first 90 days in a PM role are the most important learning period of a PM's career — and the most dangerous time to ship the wrong thing. This reading synthesises the documented advice from senior PMs on what to prioritise in the first 30, 60, and 90 days: the listening and learning phase (Days 1-30), the first contribution (Days 31-60), and the first full ownership cycle (Days 61-90). The framing is specifically relevant to APMs joining an existing team at a mid-stage Indian startup.
keyTakeaways: Days 1-30: the PM's job is to listen more than talk — map the product, the users, the metrics, the team, and the existing roadmap before forming opinions about what should change

The most common first-90-days mistake: shipping a change in the first 30 days to prove value; early shipping before fully understanding the system usually creates downstream problems that cost more time than the early win was worth
By Day 60, a PM should be able to answer: what is our North Star metric, what moves it, and what are the top 3 things currently blocking it? If you cannot answer this, you have not yet done the job

The first full ownership cycle (Days 61-90) means: running a discovery sprint, writing a PRD or user story set, getting engineering alignment, and tracking the first metric movement — not just shipping code

At Indian startups, the 90-day period also includes building relationships with founders and senior stakeholders: one insight shared in a product review that turns out to be correct is worth more than 10 correct PRDs for your long-term credibility

tasks.promptTitle: OKR audit and final APM OKR set: close the OKR thread from Day 15
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM and OKR coach.

Role: You are helping me close the OKR thread from Day 15 by writing the final, polished OKR set I would actually pursue in my first 90 days as an APM. This is not a generic exercise — it should reflect everything I have learned across 21 days.

On Day 15, I wrote my first OKRs. Today I need to revisit them with 6 days of additional learning:
- Day 16: Indian product landscape — CRED vs PhonePe competitive dynamics
- Day 17: Bharat vs India — designing for the next 400 million users
- Day 18: Case practice, API basics, and timed product design + estimation
- Day 19: Stakeholder management, managing up, and influencing without authority
- Day 20: Product quality, launch readiness, and the PM's role in shipping

Activity steps:

Step 1 — OKR audit (10 minutes)
Share the OKRs I wrote on Day 15 [Pranav pastes them here]. Review them against these criteria:
(a) Is the Objective inspiring and directional without being a metric?
(b) Are the Key Results measurable, time-bound, and genuinely leading indicators of the Objective — not just activity metrics ('ship 3 features') or lagging vanity metrics ('get 1 million users')?
(c) Are the OKRs realistic for a first 90 days, given that an APM in Days 1-30 should be listening more than shipping?
(d) Do the OKRs reflect India-specific context — i.e., would they be different if the company were in the US?

Step 2 — Revision (10 minutes)
Based on the audit, write a revised OKR set. Structure:
- 1 Objective
- 3 Key Results

The Objective should reflect the APM's primary goal in the first quarter. The Key Results should represent the specific, measurable milestones that prove the Objective is being achieved — not just completed tasks.

Step 3 — Stretch KR (5 minutes)
Add one 'stretch' Key Result — the KR that you include knowing it is ambitious but that, if achieved, would signal you outperformed in your first quarter. Mark it clearly as a stretch.

Step 4 — Stakeholder translation (5 minutes)
For each Key Result, write one sentence on how you would communicate progress on this KR to your PM lead in your weekly update — using the managing-up format from Day 19 (outcomes, not activity).

Feedback format:
WHAT WORKED: [specific OKR quality — e.g., Key Results that are genuinely leading indicators, realistic scoping for a first quarter]
WHAT MISSED: [OKR quality issues — activity metrics disguised as Key Results, Objective that is actually a task, KRs that are not measurable within the 90-day window]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about OKRs at Indian startups specifically — e.g., at many Indian Series B companies, OKRs are still aspirational documents rather than operating tools; an APM who treats OKRs as a weekly accountability mechanism will stand out]
COACHING NOTE: [one OKR habit to build before starting the APM role]

Begin with Step 1 — share the OKRs from Day 15 for the audit.

assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
This is your final integration assessment. 60 minutes. Five deliverables. No pausing to look things up.

Context:
Blinkit operates quick-commerce dark stores in 30+ cities (primarily metro and Tier 1 as of 2024). Zomato's management has publicly stated that Tier 2 expansion is a priority. You are the APM assigned to the first Tier 2 pilot — launching in 3 cities: Nashik, Surat, and Lucknow.

Challenge: Tier 2 expansion is not a copy-paste of the metro model. Key documented differences:
- Average order value (AOV) in Tier 2 is estimated to be 20-30% lower than metro
- Quick-commerce category awareness is lower — users in Tier 2 have not used 10-minute delivery before
- App usage patterns differ: more feature phones, more Hindi/regional language preference, lower average screen time
- Dark store economics require a minimum order density that is harder to reach in lower-population areas
- Existing kirana store relationships are stronger in Tier 2 — users trust local stores more than app-based delivery

Deliverable 1 — Problem statement (5 minutes)
Write a single MECE problem statement using the format: 'The problem is that [specific user] cannot [specific action] because [specific constraint] which results in [specific business or user outcome missed].' One sentence. No vague words.

Deliverable 2 — User personas (10 minutes)
Write two user personas for Blinkit Tier 2 users. Each persona: name, city, age, occupation, phone type, internet usage, current grocery behaviour, JTBD (jobs-to-be-done), and one barrier to using Blinkit today. Personas must be meaningfully different from each other — not just the same person with different cities.

Deliverable 3 — Feature options + RICE scores (15 minutes)
Write 3 feature options for the Tier 2 launch that would drive first-order conversion (the most important metric in a new market launch). For each feature:
- Feature name and one-sentence description
- RICE score: Reach (how many Tier 2 users does this affect in 90 days?), Impact (1-3 scale: how much does it move first-order conversion?), Confidence (%), Effort (person-weeks)
- RICE score = (Reach × Impact × Confidence) / Effort
After scoring, state which feature you prioritise and why in one sentence.

Deliverable 4 — OKRs for Q1 of the Tier 2 pilot (10 minutes)
Write 1 Objective and 3 Key Results for your first quarter on the Tier 2 expansion. The Objective should reflect what success looks like for the pilot, not for the full expansion. The Key Results should be specific to Tier 2 dynamics — not a copy of the metro OKRs.

Deliverable 5 — Managing-up memo (10 minutes)
Your PM lead has asked for a 2-paragraph update before the weekly product review. Paragraph 1: current status of the Tier 2 pilot plan (use the outputs from Deliverables 1-4 as your evidence). Paragraph 2: the one thing you need from your PM lead in the next week — specific, time-bound, and framed as an outcome ask not an activity request.

Self-score after completing all 5 deliverables, before pasting into Claude.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Five labelled deliverables. Deliverable 1: 1 sentence. Deliverable 2: 2 personas, each with 7-8 fields. Deliverable 3: 3 features with full RICE breakdown and a final prioritisation sentence. Deliverable 4: 1 Objective + 3 Key Results. Deliverable 5: 2 paragraphs (≤80 words each). Total: aim for 700-900 words.

You are a senior PM reviewing my Day 21 final integration assessment — the culmination of a 21-day APM foundations programme.

Real world context I should apply:
Blinkit Tier 2 expansion — launching 10-minute quick-commerce in Nashik, Surat, and Lucknow. Challenges: lower AOV, lower category awareness, more feature phones, Hindi/regional language preference, stronger kirana store trust. First priority metric: first-order conversion.

Are the RICE scores internally consistent (does the Reach estimate match the pilot scope of 3 cities)? Do the OKRs reflect Tier 2 dynamics or are they generic growth OKRs?
